descriptions.claim: The 2011 paper attributes the classic haloing/gradient-reversal failure mode of edge-aware pyramid processing specifically to naive per-coefficient rescaling of Laplacian pyramid coefficients, and defines edge-awareness as a requirement that edge transitions not become smoother or sharper.
descriptions.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, 'Local Laplacian Filters,' SIGGRAPH/TOG 2011, Sec 1 (Intro) and Sec 3: 'Failure to account for edges in these applications leads to distracting visual artifacts such as haloing, shifted edges, or reversals of gradients' (p.1, col 2); 'A first approach is to directly rescale the coefficients of a Laplacian pyramid, however, this typically produces halos [Li et al. 2005]' (Related Work, p.1)
descriptions.detail: The paper's own edge-aware criterion (Sec 3, intro paragraph): output I' must keep large discontinuities (edges) in place with unchanged profile shape -- amplitude may change but 'the edge transitions should not become smoother or sharper.' Any operator that violates this locally is defined as producing the haloing/shifted-edge/gradient-reversal artifact class.

descriptions.claim: LLF's halo avoidance mechanism is the detail-vs-edge piecewise remapping split at threshold sigma_r, formalized by two constrained functions r_d (details, |i-g0|<=sigma_r) and r_e (edges, |i-g0|>sigma_r) that must agree in value at the boundary g0 +/- sigma_r (C0-continuity) to avoid spurious discontinuities.
descriptions.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, SIGGRAPH 2011, Sec 4, Eq. 1-2: r_d(i) = g0 + sign(i-g0) * sigma_r * f_d(|i-g0|/sigma_r); r_e(i) = g0 + sign(i-g0) * (f_e(|i-g0|-sigma_r) + sigma_r); constraint 'r_d(g0 +/- sigma_r) = r_e(g0 +/- sigma_r)'
descriptions.detail: r(i) = r_d(i) if |i-g0|<=sigma_r else r_e(i), with r required monotonically increasing overall (both r_d and r_e monotonic) 'to avoid the creation of spurious discontinuities.' Color version in Eq. 3a-3b uses unit(i-g0) and the 2-norm ||i-g0|| in place of sign/abs for RGB/Lab vectors.

descriptions.claim: For tone mapping specifically, the edge function is fe(a) = beta*a (linear slope beta on the portion of intensity difference above sigma_r), with beta<1 compressing dynamic range and beta>1 expanding it (inverse tone mapping); detail function fd(Delta)=Delta^alpha with alpha<=1 used to preserve/enhance detail during compression.
descriptions.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, SIGGRAPH 2011, Sec 5.3: 'we manipulate large-scale variations by defining a point-wise function modifying the edge amplitude, fe(a) = beta*a, where beta>=0 is a user-defined parameter'; 'For tone mapping, we set our filter with alpha<=1 ... and beta<1 so that edges are compressed'
descriptions.detail: Also gives fd(Delta)=Delta^alpha for detail manipulation (Sec 5.2) with a noise-guard blend fd(Delta)=tau*Delta^alpha+(1-tau)*Delta where tau is a smoothstep zero below 1% of max intensity and one above 2%, used in ALL results in the paper to limit noise amplification when alpha<1.

descriptions.claim: In tone mapping, LLF operates on a single derived log-intensity channel (not per-RGB-channel), leaving color ratios untouched, then remaps the filtered log intensity to a fixed 100:1 display dynamic range using robust 99.5th/0.5th percentile min/max before gamma correction (1/2.2).
descriptions.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, SIGGRAPH 2011, Sec 5.3: intensity Ii = (1/61)(20*Ir+40*Ig+Ib), color ratios (rho_r,rho_g,rho_b)=(1/Ii)(Ir,Ig,Ib); 'we estimate a robust maximum and minimum with the 99.5th and 0.5th percentiles, and we set the scale factor so that the output dynamic range is 100:1 for the linear intensities'
descriptions.detail: This is the mechanism for mapping the filtered log(Ii') back to displayable [0,1]: offset to max=0, then scale by the percentile-derived factor, multiply back by color ratios, gamma-correct 1/2.2. Recommended default sigma_r = log(2.5), advised to keep sigma_r fixed and vary beta between 0 (local contrast dominates) and 1 (global contrast dominates).

descriptions.claim: The 2011 paper provides no discrete-intensity-sampling (N-level) approximation scheme at all -- that mechanism (and its accuracy/quantization analysis) is entirely absent from the original SIGGRAPH 2011 paper and first appears in the 2014 Fast Local Laplacian Filters follow-up.
descriptions.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, SIGGRAPH 2011, full text search
descriptions.detail: grep for 'quantiz', 'discret', 'banding', 'number of levels', 'sampled', 'interpolat' in the extracted paper text returns zero hits related to an intensity-domain sampling scheme; the paper's only speed optimization is limiting sub-pyramid DEPTH (Sec 5.1: 'limiting the depth of the intermediate pyramid to at most 5 levels ... applying the remapping to level max(0, l0-3)'), not intensity-domain discretization. PSNR of that depth-limiting approximation is reported as 'on the order of 30 to 40 dB.'

descriptions.claim: The 2011 paper explicitly documents its own semantic (non-edge-related) failure mode: large detail-enhancement gains can amplify texture the filter cannot distinguish from meaningful edges, e.g. sky-behind-clouds locally darkening because it 'forms a blue-white texture amplified by our filter' -- a signal-based artifact unrelated to halos, shown as a documented failure case.
descriptions.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, SIGGRAPH 2011, Sec 5.4 (Discussion) and Fig. 13 caption: 'Our approach is purely signal-based and its ignorance of scene semantics can lead to artifacts... the sky gets locally darker behind clouds, because it forms a blue-white texture amplified by our filter'
descriptions.detail: Also: 'our method can fail in the presence of excessive noise or when extreme parameter settings are used (e.g., the lenna picture in supplemental material has a high level of noise)' -- one of the only two explicit failure-case admissions in the paper.

descriptions.claim: The user's artifact class 2 ('pools of adjustment around small bright regions inside lifted dark regions') maps precisely onto the 'compartmentalization effect' defined and measured by Hessel & Morel: an intensity-shift-of-the-detail-layer artifact inside constant-color regions whose magnitude scales inversely with the region's spatial area (smaller region -> stronger shift), measured via P(u0,u1) = var{(u0-u1) * 1_inter} over a bright-squares-on-dark-background test pattern (Eq. 14).
descriptions.source: Hessel & Morel, arXiv:1808.09411, Sec IV-C: 'The compartmentalization effect arises when a constant color region ... is compartmented into pieces with variable size ... The artifact consists in an intensity shift of the detail in constant regions; its magnitude depends on the area of the region. The smaller the region, the stronger the artifact.' Eq. 14: P(u0,u1) = var{(u0-u1) 1_inter}
descriptions.detail: Test pattern: bright squares/rectangles of varying area on a dark background (Fig. 2/5/6); measured on a mask 1_inter that excludes a 2-pixel band along inner/outer shape borders to avoid conflating with the separate luminance-halo artifact. In the paper's ranking, WLS (Weighted Least Squares) and Domain Transform showed the worst compartmentalization scores; this is NOT one of the four artifacts named in the original LLF/Fast-LLF papers -- it is this follow-up's own taxonomy term, useful as descriptive vocabulary for the user's 'pooling' observation even though neither Paris 2011 nor Aubry 2014 uses this term.

descriptions.claim: The 2014 paper directly documents that intermediate pyramid depths (not the shallowest 1-2 levels, nor the full/deep pyramid) are where halos become visible when varying the number of LLF pyramid levels used, cross-validated against an independent perceptual study (Trentacoste et al. 2012).
descriptions.source: Aubry, Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, Durand, TOG 2014, Sec 3, p.6-7 and Fig. 7 caption: 'Figure 7 shows the effects of varying the pyramid depth to transition from the unnormalized bilateral filter with one level to local Laplacian filtering with a full pyramid. Halos may be observed at intermediate depths whereas they are typically not an issue at small and large scales. This observation is consistent with the findings of Trentacoste et al. [2012].' Fig. 7 caption: 'Unsightly halos are visible at intermediate depths (c) [4 levels]; this observation is consistent with the results of Trentacoste et al. [2012]'
descriptions.detail: Fig. 7 shows a-d = 1 level (unnormalized bilateral filter), 2 levels, 4 levels, 8 levels, sigma_r=0.05 fixed for all. The cited follow-up work is Trentacoste, Mantiuk, Heidrich, Dufrot, 'Unsharp Masking, Countershading and Halos: Enhancements or Artifacts?', Eurographics/Computer Graphics Forum 31(2), 555-564, 2012 -- a perceptual study of countershading-profile-width vs perceived enhancement/artifact, referenced twice in the 2014 paper (also for its own halo evaluation methodology on test images, per the paper's Related Work discussion of prior halo-measurement work).

descriptions.claim: The 2014 paper identifies the mathematical origin of halos/gradient-reversal in bilateral-style filters as the NORMALIZATION term (1/W_p): local Laplacian filters differ from bilateral filtering chiefly by being UNNORMALIZED, and the paper's own 'unnormalized bilateral filter' variant (removing normalization while keeping the bilateral filter's single-scale design) measurably reduces edge artifacts/spurious detail-layer content relative to the standard normalized bilateral filter.
descriptions.source: Aubry, Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, Durand, TOG 2014, Sec 2.1.3 and Sec 2.2, Fig. 5 caption: 'These spurious variations are a source of halos and gradient reversals in applications such as tone mapping. In comparison, the unnormalized filter produces a cleaner edge with almost no spurious content in the detail layer.'
descriptions.detail: Eq. 14 defines UBF_p = I_p + sum_q G_sigma_s(q-p) G_sigma_r(I_q-I_p)(I_q-I_p) (no 1/W_p normalizer, contrast with the normalized bilateral filter Eq. 4a/6). Eq. 15 shows UBF as a spatially-varying blend UBF_p=(1-alpha_p)*I_p + alpha_p*BF_p where alpha_p = sum_q G_sigma_s(q-p)G_sigma_r(I_q-I_p) is close to 1 in uniform regions and drops near edges -- explaining why the unnormalized variant has a weaker (softer, less artifact-prone) effect specifically at discontinuities. Both standard AND unnormalized bilateral filters 'can generate reverse gradients on thin features (e.g., on the window frame)' (Fig 6) while 'local Laplacian filters are not prone to this problem' because of their genuinely multiscale (not two-scale) construction.

descriptions.claim: darktable's local-laplacian residual/coarsest pyramid band (output[last_level]) is copied straight from a single gauss_reduce of the ORIGINAL (uncurved) padded input, never touched by curve_scalar() or any per-gamma remapping — the absolute-shift/pooling artifact class the user is chasing cannot originate in the base band of darktable's own implementation, only in the per-level Laplacian coefficients added on top of it.
descriptions.source: src/common/locallaplacian.c, local_laplacian_internal(): line 441 `gauss_reduce(padded[last_level-1], output[last_level], dl(w,last_level-1), dl(h,last_level-1));` runs BEFORE the num_gamma loop that builds buf[k][l] from curve_scalar-processed images (lines 467-474); output[last_level] is only overwritten afterward if b->mode==2 (OpenCL preview interpolation, lines 479-534), otherwise it passes through unchanged into the coarse-to-fine reconstruction loop at line 537.

descriptions.claim: GitHub issue #3418 ('local contrast: wrong colors in 100% view'): at high Detail values (350-500%) the local-laplacian result visibly darkens/brightens depending on preview zoom level and whether side panels are shown (i.e. depending on the *rendered raster size* of the preview pipe). Developer hanatos's closing explanation: 'local laplacian is a frequency filter, its output depends on the image scale. The reference is zoomed at 100%, any other zoom level is merely informative. Not a bug.' This is a precise, citable statement that scale-dependent output (hence apparent 'pooling'/brightness drift around features whose size is comparable to the pyramid's coarse levels) is an acknowledged, unfixed structural property of the algorithm as shipped, not a bug they intend to patch.
descriptions.source: https://github.com/darktable-org/darktable/issues/3418 (issue body + hanatos comment)

descriptions.claim: The scale-dependence has a concrete numeric mechanism in code: darktable's local-contrast module rescales the algorithm's spatial sigma by the current pixelpipe zoom before running, `const float scale = fmaxf(piece->iscale / roi_in->scale, 1.f); ... const float sigma_s = d->sigma_s / scale;` — meaning the preview pipe (rendered at less than 100%, so roi_in->scale<1, scale>1) and the full-res/export pipe (roi_in->scale==1, scale==1) use DIFFERENT effective sigma_s, which is the root cause behind issues #3418 and #4080's preview-vs-export color mismatches.
descriptions.source: src/iop/bilat.c, process() line 341-343 (comment: 'used to adjust blur level depending on size. Don't amplify noise if magnified > 100%')

descriptions.claim: PR #1403 (hanatos, original local-laplacian merge, 'local contrast via local laplacian filters') lists as a known limitation at merge time: 'region of interest handling is not really implemented, cropped versions may look different' — an explicit developer admission that the algorithm's boundary/padding handling is scale- and crop-dependent, i.e. the same neighbourhood of pixels can produce different Laplacian coefficients (and hence different local contrast/shadow-lift amounts) depending on what portion of the image was fed to the pyramid.
descriptions.source: https://github.com/darktable-org/darktable/pull/1403 (PR description)

descriptions.claim: Confirmed directly in source: darktable's local-laplacian mode explicitly disables pixelpipe tiling — `if(d->mode == s_mode_local_laplacian) piece->process_tiling_ready = FALSE; // can't deal with tiles, sorry.` — because the algorithm needs the whole image (full support up to max_supp = 1<<last_level) to compute correct pyramid boundaries; tiling would reintroduce exactly the boundary-condition artifacts the whole-image pass is designed to avoid.
descriptions.source: src/iop/bilat.c, commit_params() line 307-308; tiling_callback() line 279-289 sets tiling->overlap = rad where rad = MIN(roi_in->width, ceilf(256 * roi_in->scale / piece->iscale))

descriptions.claim: GitHub issue #3756 ('amplified effect' on AMD ROCm) contains an empirical pyramid-buffer trace from user RvRijsselt showing errors originate at the coarsest level and propagate outward: 'The coarsest (L9, 10x9 pixels) output of ROCm seems to introduce some darker patch in the top right area. This then affects all finer levels and results in block like artifacts.' This confirms, via direct pyramid-buffer inspection, that an error introduced at the coarsest/residual band is NOT locally contained but spreads globally through every finer gauss_expand() step of the coarse-to-fine reconstruction.
descriptions.source: https://github.com/darktable-org/darktable/issues/3756 (RvRijsselt comment with dumped pyramid-level comparison image)

descriptions.claim: A pixls.us forum thread ('Experimenting with Local Laplacian Filters,' users Carmelo/afre et al.) independently reimplementing the algorithm reports the same two artifact classes the user described: 'edge darkening... suspected to be a boundary condition problem related to pyramid padding' and 'highlight flattening... attributed to an overly simplistic remapping function,' and identifies the root technical question as 'how to properly select the region in the high-resolution image that needs to be re-mapped in order to correctly estimate a given laplacian coefficient in a given layer' — i.e. support-window/footprint selection at each pyramid level is the crux of both halo and flattening artifacts; the thread also confirms truncating the pyramid before it collapses to a single pixel (rather than going 'down to the smallest level, in which the image is squeezed into a single pixel') introduces further artifacts.
descriptions.source: https://discuss.pixls.us/t/experimenting-with-local-laplacian-filters/13749

descriptions.claim: The Laplacian coefficient at each pyramid level is computed as a difference against a re-expanded coarse level using a fixed 5-tap [1,4,6,4,1]/256 binomial kernel — ll_laplacian() calls ll_expand_gaussian() which implements the classic Burt-Adelson 4-case (even/odd row-column parity) interpolation stencil with weights 6/256, 24/256, 4/256, and 1/4 — meaning the effective support radius at pyramid level l is a small, fixed few-pixel binomial footprint at that level's resolution, not a large-radius edge-aware kernel. This is consistent with 'pooling' artifacts around a small bright feature in a large dark region: only once the pyramid reaches a coarse enough level that the feature becomes sub-pixel does a single shared coefficient cover the feature AND its dark surroundings together, at which point curve-driven adjustment of that coefficient shifts both in lockstep.
descriptions.source: src/common/locallaplacian.c, ll_expand_gaussian() lines 46-84, ll_laplacian() lines 300-311

descriptions.claim: The guided filter is provably free of gradient-reversal artifacts (unlike the bilateral filter) but is NOT free of halos -- these are two distinct failure modes, and the paper's own 1-D figure shows the mechanism: bilateral filter detail layer has 'great fluctuations' near an edge because too few similar pixels exist there, causing the recombined signal to show visibly reversed gradients, whereas the guided filter base layer obeys nabla q ~= a_bar * nabla I so gradient sign is preserved even though a halo (a smooth transition band) can still exist.
descriptions.detail: Section 3.4 'Gradient Preserving Filtering': 'Though the guided filter is an edge-preserving smoothing filter like the bilateral filter, it avoids the gradient reversal artifacts that may appear in detail enhancement and HDR compression... For the bilateral filter, the base layer is not consistent with input signal at the edge pixels. This is because few pixels around them have similar colors, and the Gaussian weighted average has little statistical data and becomes unreliable. So the detail layer has great fluctuations, and the recombined signal has reversed gradients... On the other hand, the guided filter... better preserves the gradient information in I, because the gradient of the base layer is nabla q ~= a_bar * nabla I near the edge. The shape of the edge is well maintained in the recombined layer.' This is a mechanistic distinction directly reusable in a test suite: gradient-reversal-freedom (sign of local gradient unchanged) is a strictly weaker/different property than halo-freedom (no visible over/undershoot band).
descriptions.source: He, Sun, Tang, 'Guided Image Filtering' (ECCV 2010), Section 3.4 and Figure 4 (1-D illustration for detail enhancement, bilateral vs guided)

descriptions.claim: Farbman/Fattal/Lischinski/Szeliski (WLS, SIGGRAPH 2008) attribute halos and gradient reversals to a fundamental tradeoff in the bilateral filter between its spatial support sigma_s and its range support sigma_r: increasing sigma_s alone re-introduces fine-scale noise near edges (because in the limit sigma_s->infinity the BLF degenerates into a pure range filter), while increasing sigma_r alone to achieve coarser smoothing degrades edge preservation (in the limit sigma_r->infinity the BLF degenerates into a linear Gaussian filter) -- meaning progressive multi-scale coarsening with a single bilateral filter is structurally halo-prone.
descriptions.detail: Exact text: 'this example demonstrates that the BLF trades off its edge-preservation abilities with its smoothing abilities. As the scale of the extracted details increases, the BLF tends to blur over more edges, which may produce halo artifacts.' Demonstrated with a synthetic step-edge test image (Figure 3) filtered at sigma_s={4,12}, sigma_r={0.15,0.3,0.45}, contrasted against their WLS operator at alpha={1.2,1.8}, lambda={0.25,0.35} which achieves both finer and coarser smoothing while preserving step edges 'without introducing obvious artifacts.' They also state the general principle (Fig 2 caption, after Tumblin & Turk 1999): 'Boosting the details... and recombining with the base results in halos and gradient reversals' whenever the coarsened/base signal either blurs OR sharpens an edge relative to the true edge profile -- i.e. halos are not unique to blurring; oversharpening a base layer produces the same artifact class.
descriptions.source: Farbman, Fattal, Lischinski, Szeliski, 'Edge-Preserving Decompositions for Multi-Scale Tone and Detail Manipulation' (SIGGRAPH 2008), Section 2 ('Background') and Figure 2/Figure 3

descriptions.claim: Aubry et al. 2014 report that halos in local Laplacian filtering appear specifically at INTERMEDIATE pyramid depths, not at the smallest or largest scales, when transitioning from a two-level (unnormalized bilateral) filter to a full multi-level pyramid -- a specific, falsifiable claim about where in scale-space halos concentrate.
descriptions.detail: Exact text: 'Figure 7 shows the effects of varying the pyramid depth to transition from the unnormalized bilateral filter with one level to local Laplacian filtering with a full pyramid. Halos may be observed at intermediate depths whereas they are typically not an issue at small and large scales. This observation is consistent with the findings of Trentacoste et al. [2012].' This is directly actionable for a test suite targeting an operator that adds a calibrated curve to the pyramid RESIDUAL at ~64px scale: the 64px scale is an intermediate scale (not the finest detail level, not the coarsest residual), which is exactly the regime this paper flags as halo-prone.
descriptions.source: Aubry, Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, Durand, 'Fast Local Laplacian Filters...' (TOG 2014), Section 3 'Efficient Local Laplacian Filtering', discussion after Figure 7/Figure 9

descriptions.claim: Paris/Hasinoff/Kautz 2011 explicitly document a 'pooling'-adjacent failure mode: small, high-frequency textured regions (their example: sky texture behind clouds) get locally over-darkened/amplified by the detail-enhancing filter because the filter has no semantic understanding and treats any locally high-variance region as 'detail to amplify' regardless of whether it constitutes a legitimate scene feature -- this is the closest primary-source description of the 'pools of adjustment around small bright/dark regions' artifact class.
descriptions.detail: Exact text (Figure 13 caption and Section 5.4 Discussion): 'For a large increase in local contrast (b), at a level similar to Figure 17, the sky gets locally darker behind clouds, because it forms a blue-white texture amplified by our filter... While our method can fail in the presence of excessive noise or when extreme parameter settings are used... A limitation of our approach is the lack of semantic understanding of the scene. For instance, the method can sometimes introduce unnatural variations in a uniform sky or darken regions that one would expect to be lit... One typical difficulty we encountered is that sometimes the sky interacts with other elements to form high-frequency textures that undesirably get amplified by our detail-enhancing filter... Such misinterpretation is common to all low-level filters without semantic understanding of the scene, and typically requires user feedback to correct.' The mechanism is: filter support size vs. feature size mismatch combined with the remapping function's blind amplification of any |i-g0| within sigma_r, regardless of whether that local variation is due to real texture, noise, or a small isolated region embedded in a larger tonally-different area.
descriptions.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, 'Local Laplacian Filters...' (SIGGRAPH 2011), Figure 13 caption and Section 5.4 'Discussion'

descriptions.claim: Fast Local Laplacian Filters (Aubry et al. 2014) reports that both the standard and unnormalized bilateral filters CAN still generate reversed gradients specifically on thin features (their example: a window frame), whereas local Laplacian filters were not observed to be prone to this -- a distinction between 'edge halos' (which the unnormalized variant fixes) and 'thin-feature gradient reversal' (which only the full multi-scale LLF avoids), directly relevant to distinguishing halo classes 1 and 3 in the user's own taxonomy.
descriptions.detail: Exact text: 'both versions of the bilateral filter can generate reverse gradients on thin features (e.g., on the window frame) while the local Laplacian filters are not prone to this problem. Further, we found that the local Laplacian filters can generate stronger effects because it affects several frequency bands unlike the unnormalized bilateral filter that alters details at a single scale.' This implies thin, isolated bright/dark features (a strip, a thin highlight) are a distinct stress case from broad step edges, and single-scale filters (bilateral, unnormalized bilateral, guided filter) are structurally worse on them than genuinely multi-scale operators (WLS, LLF) regardless of their per-scale halo behavior.
descriptions.source: Aubry, Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, Durand, 'Fast Local Laplacian Filters...' (TOG 2014), Section 2.2 'Unnormalized bilateral filtering', discussion paragraph after Eq.15

descriptions.claim: The original Local Laplacian Filters paper names three specific artifact classes produced by edge-unaware multiscale/pyramid manipulation: 'haloing', 'shifted edges', and 'reversals of gradients' -- exactly the taxonomy the user is asking about.
descriptions.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, 'Local Laplacian Filters: Edge-aware Image Processing with a Laplacian Pyramid', ACM TOG 30(4), 2011, Section 3 intro ('Dealing with Edges in Laplacian Pyramids'): 'Failure to account for edges in these applications leads to distracting visual artifacts such as haloing, shifted edges, or reversals of gradients.'
descriptions.detail: This is the field's canonical three-way taxonomy. 'Gradient reversal' specifically names sign-flips in the local derivative at/near an edge -- i.e. a bright band appearing where the signal should still be descending, which matches the user's H1 'exposure halo' class. This taxonomy long predates and generalizes the user's own H1/H2/H3 split.

descriptions.claim: Naive coefficient truncation/rescaling in a Laplacian pyramid (the intuitive 'just clip the big coefficients' approach) produces 'thin but unsightly rim halos' -- a specific, named sub-type of halo that is spatially narrow (a 'rim') rather than a broad glow.
descriptions.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz 2011, Section 3.2 ('Pyramid-based Range Compression'): 'Such deformations typically affect only a small band of pixels, manifested in images as thin but unsightly rim halos.'
descriptions.detail: This is distinguished in the paper from the wider 'pooling'/glow-type halo the user describes for H2 -- the paper's figure (Fig. 3) shows the mechanism: truncating large Laplacian coefficients smooths/rounds the edge profile, producing 'wider lobes' in the reconstructed signal versus the sharp original.

descriptions.claim: The paper gives an explicit failure-mode figure/finding for 'semantic misinterpretation' halos distinct from edge halos: strong detail-boost settings (alpha=0.25) cause the sky to 'get locally darker behind clouds, because it forms a blue-white texture amplified by our filter' -- i.e. texture in a region with no hard edge gets mistakenly amplified as if it were edge detail, producing a diffuse tonal artifact.
descriptions.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz 2011, Figure 13 caption and Section 5.4 ('Discussion'): 'Our approach is purely signal-based and its ignorance of scene semantics can lead to artifacts... this 'misinterpretation' is common to all low-level filters without semantic understanding of the scene.'
descriptions.detail: This is the closest documented analogue in the primary LLF literature to the user's H2 'pooling' artifact (adjustment glow around small bright regions inside a lifted dark zone): a region gets mis-classified by the sigma_r threshold as detail/texture rather than a flat area, so the point-wise remap amplifies/depresses it independent of its surrounding large-scale context.

descriptions.claim: The core LLF remapping function is r(i) = rd(i) for |i-g0| <= sigma_r (detail arm) and re(i) for |i-g0| > sigma_r (edge arm), with rd(i) = g0 + sign(i-g0)*sigma_r*fd(|i-g0|/sigma_r) using fd(delta)=delta^alpha (alpha>0), and re(i) = g0 + sign(i-g0)*(fe(|i-g0|-sigma_r)+sigma_r) using fe(a)=beta*a (beta>=0). alpha<1 boosts detail contrast, alpha>1 smooths detail; beta<1 compresses edge amplitude (tone mapping), beta=1 leaves edges untouched, beta>1 expands them (inverse tone mapping).
descriptions.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz 2011, Equations 1-2 and Section 5.2-5.3 ('Detail Manipulation', 'Tone Manipulation'); Figure 6 shows the six-way family of r(i) shapes parametrized by alpha, beta.
descriptions.detail: For tone mapping specifically the paper sets alpha<=1 (preserve/enhance detail) and beta<1 (compress edges/global contrast), operating on log(luminance). The paper explicitly recommends fixing sigma_r=log(2.5) and varying beta between 0 (local contrast dominates dynamic range) and 1 (global contrast dominates) as the practical tuning knob -- directly relevant to calibrating an absolute tone curve injected at the residual/coarse level, since beta there plays the analogous 'how much of the total range compression happens locally vs globally' role.

descriptions.claim: The paper gives NO closed-form theorem bounding alpha/beta to guarantee zero gradient reversal; the 'halo-free' claim in Paris et al. 2011 is empirical/qualitative ('none of them exhibit halos... halo-free results can indeed be obtained'), not a proven numeric bound. It is the continuity constraint rd(g0+-sigma_r)=re(g0+-sigma_r) and monotonicity of r that are formally required (to avoid spurious discontinuities), not a slope/derivative bound.
descriptions.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz 2011, Section 5 ('Applications and Results') closing text and Section 3.2 discussion: 'To satisfy this basic requirement, it is sufficient for the remapping function to be monotonically increasing.'
descriptions.detail: Important for calibration: do not expect a citable alpha/beta inequality from this paper. The formal halo-avoidance mechanism is architectural (per-coefficient full-resolution re-rendering, see next finding), not a parameter bound. The project's own docs/archive/research/v10c-local-laplacian-base-deferred.md correctly notes this guarantee (there cited as 'Paris 2011 §5') is a property of the paper's own construction, not a portable slope bound.

descriptions.claim: Google's HDR+ system (Hasinoff et al. 2016, SIGGRAPH Asia) explicitly names its shipped, unfixed local-tone-mapping failure mode 'medium frequency halos', gives a concrete visual signature ('dark blue patches in sky'), and states the root cause: 'due to our use of exposure fusion' on high-contrast scenes.
descriptions.source: Hasinoff, Sharlet, Geiss, Adams, Barron, Kainz, Chen, Levoy, 'Burst photography for high dynamic range and low-light imaging on mobile cameras', ACM TOG 35(6) / SIGGRAPH Asia 2016, Figure 11 caption ('Limitations and failure cases') and Section 7 ('Results') / 'Failure cases'.
descriptions.detail: Full caption: 'High contrast scenes can exhibit mild medium frequency halos (dark blue patches in sky) due to our use of exposure fusion.' This is a named, shipped, production-acknowledged failure mode from a system deployed to millions of users -- useful precedent that even Adobe/Google-caliber production local tone mapping does not achieve zero-halo, and that halos are explicitly frequency-classified (the paper distinguishes this from low-frequency 'excessive range compression' cartoony artifacts, Figure 2c) and scene-classified (high-contrast scenes specifically).

descriptions.claim: HDR+'s local tone mapping is architecturally a two-image (not multi-bracket) exposure fusion: it synthesizes exactly two 'synthetic exposures' (one short, one long) from a single merged HDR intermediate via gain+gamma, fuses them grayscale-only using a FIXED one-dimensional lookup-table weighting function 'that favors moderately bright pixels' (not the full multiplicative C*S*E product of Mertens et al.), then re-colorizes by copying per-pixel chroma ratios from the original linear RGB image after undoing the gamma correction.
descriptions.source: Hasinoff et al. 2016, Section 6 ('Finishing') / 'Dynamic range compression' paragraph.
descriptions.detail: Grayscale-only fusion + chroma-ratio recolorization is itself a halo/pooling-avoidance design choice: by keeping saturation/contrast weighting off (only well-exposedness-like brightness weighting survives, as a 1D LUT) and reapplying original chroma ratios afterward, HDR+ avoids the specific tonal-pooling failure that a full multiplicative weight product (as in Mertens) can produce around small saturated/bright regions -- directly relevant to the user's H2 'pools around small bright regions' artifact, since Mertens-style saturation/contrast weighting is exactly what would locally spike a small bright patch's fusion weight and pull the surrounding compression toward it.

descriptions.claim: Mertens et al. 2007 also document a residual, unsolved 'blurred seam' artifact in their own multiresolution method: 'One of the results (see supplemental video) contains a spurious low frequency brightness change, which... can be considered as a very blurred version of the seam problem' -- i.e. even Laplacian-pyramid blending does not perfectly eliminate low-frequency pooling artifacts, it only pushes the residual seam/pooling error to very low spatial frequency (large-scale, diffuse) rather than eliminating it.
descriptions.source: Mertens, Kautz, Van Reeth 2007, Section 4.1 ('Quality') discussion of limitations.
descriptions.detail: This is a useful negative-result finding: multi-scale weighting reliably converts sharp seams into low-frequency 'pooling'/glow-type residuals rather than removing the underlying tension entirely -- suggesting that if the user's operator still shows H2-style pooling after moving to a multi-scale weighted scheme, that is a documented residual failure mode of the mitigation itself, not evidence the mitigation was implemented wrong.

descriptions.claim: Adobe Camera Raw/Lightroom's Highlights and Shadows sliders are officially described by Adobe's own Principal Scientist as using 'local adaptation': 'the controls do different things in different parts of the image... it's as if each pixel has its own tone curve', explicitly contrasted with the Tone Curve panel's highlights/shadows region, which is a purely global/direct adjustment that 'act[s] globally, doing the same thing at every pixel'; Highlights/Shadows/Clarity/Dehaze are also documented as jointly capable of producing 'bright or dark halos around the edges of areas where abrupt changes in brightness are found', with the note that pre-Process-Version-2012 Recovery/Fill sliders 'really did make noticeable halos with extreme adjustments' but the current (local-Laplacian-based) implementation is 'very well-behaved in that respect'.
descriptions.source: Eric Chan (Adobe Senior Principal Scientist, Camera Raw), cited via secondary summary in web search results referencing Adobe documentation/forum material; corroborated by Adobe's official help page 'Make color and tonal adjustments in Adobe Camera Raw' (helpx.adobe.com/camera-raw/using/make-color-tonal-adjustments-camera.html).
descriptions.detail: Chan is listed by Adobe's own author page as the Camera Raw scientist responsible for 'Highlights and Shadows, Clarity, Dehaze, Camera Profiles, Lens Corrections, Upright, and GPU acceleration', and Eric Chan is a co-author alongside Sylvain Paris (also Adobe) on the 2011 Local Laplacian Filters paper -- strongly suggesting (though Adobe does not publicly confirm the exact production algorithm) that Camera Raw's Highlights/Shadows since Process Version 2012 (Lightroom 4) is LLF-derived or LLF-adjacent. This is Adobe's own public framing of exactly the halo problem the user is investigating, confirming 'local adaptation' and 'halo' are the terms Adobe itself uses publicly, though it stops short of disclosing algorithmic mitigation details.

descriptions.claim: A CreativePro.com technical article on Lightroom/ACR Highlights/Shadows explicitly frames the mechanism in classic darkroom terms: the local-adaptation edge-contrast boost is functionally the same effect as film-darkroom 'acutance' (edge-enhancement), stating 'the value of a shade changes faster as it approaches its border with another shade... we perceive this edge contrast as improved retention of detail after an adjustment.'
descriptions.source: CreativePro Network, 'Editing Highlights and Shadows in Adobe Lightroom and Camera Raw' (creativepro.com/editing-highlights-and-shadows-adobe-lightroom-and-camera-raw/).
descriptions.detail: This is a plain-language, non-Adobe-official secondary description that connects the local-adaptation mechanism to the acutance/unsharp-masking family -- useful vocabulary bridge but does not give algorithmic or numeric detail, and does not discuss halos directly.

descriptions.claim: darktable's tone equalizer computes a per-pixel luminance/exposure map from an RGB norm of the input, then edge-aware-blurs it with a guided filter (guide image = the luminance map itself, i.e. a self-guided filter) before applying per-EV-octave gain, specifically to avoid the gradient-reversal artifacts bilateral filters produce at sharp edges.
descriptions.source: src/iop/toneequal.c, top-of-file DOCUMENTATION comment: "The details preservation modes make use of a fast guided filter optimized to perform an edge-aware surface blur on the luminance mask, in the same spirit as the bilateral filter, but without its classic issues of gradient reversal around sharp edges. This surface blur will allow to perform piece-wise smooth exposure compensation, so local contrast will be preserved inside contiguous regions."

descriptions.claim: The exact verbatim UI tooltip for the "feathering" (guided-filter epsilon-like precision) parameter states the halo-vs-selectivity tradeoff directly: higher values follow edges more closely but can void the smoothing effect; lower values smooth better but 'may lead to inaccurate edges taping and halos.' Slider range is 0.1-50 (soft), field range 0.01-10000, default 1.0.
descriptions.source:
src/iop/toneequal.c, GUI setup: `gtk_widget_set_tooltip_text(g->feathering, _("precision of the feathering:
higher values force the mask to follow edges more closely
but may void the effect of the smoothing
lower values give smoother gradients and better smoothing
but may lead to inaccurate edges taping and halos"));` soft range `dt_bauhaus_slider_set_soft_range(g->feathering, 0.1, 50.0)`.

descriptions.claim: darktable's manual explicitly names the artifact class from over-aggressive feathering/edge-following as producing halos, and names the failure mode of too-smooth (low feathering) masks the same way — both directions of the feathering slider are documented to trade off against 'halos' as the single named failure mode (no separate 'pooling' term used by darktable; the closest analog is quantization-caused 'posterisation').
descriptions.source: docs.darktable.org/usermanual/4.6/.../tone-equalizer/, feathering description: "Higher values force the mask to follow high contrast edges more closely. Lower values give smoother gradients, but may introduce halos." and quantization description: "Apply a degree of posterisation to the mask, so that it tends to centre round a few discrete levels."

descriptions.claim: In the Paris et al. 2011 local Laplacian filter formulation, the detail/edge split is a hard radius threshold on intensity distance from the local Gaussian-pyramid value g0: pixels with |i-g0| <= sigma_r are treated by the detail remap function r_d, pixels with |i-g0| > sigma_r by the edge remap function r_e, and continuity is enforced by construction requiring r_d(g0 +/- sigma_r) = r_e(g0 +/- sigma_r) — this continuity constraint at the sigma_r boundary is exactly the mechanism that prevents the piecewise remap from creating a spurious discontinuity (a halo seed) at the detail/edge crossover.
descriptions.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, "Local Laplacian Filters: Edge-aware Image Processing with a Laplacian Pyramid," SIGGRAPH 2011 (full paper PDF, people.csail.mit.edu/sparis), Section 4: 'r(i) = rd(i) if |i-g0| <= sigma_r and r(i) = re(i) otherwise... to avoid the creation of spurious discontinuities, we constrain rd and re to be continuous by requiring that rd(g0 +/- sigma_r) = re(g0 +/- sigma_r).' Equations: rd(i) = g0 + sign(i-g0) * sigma_r * fd(|i-g0|/sigma_r)  [Eq. 1]; re(i) = g0 + sign(i-g0) * fe(|i-g0| - sigma_r) + sigma_r  [Eq. 2].

descriptions.claim: The theoretical justification the local-Laplacian family gives for being halo-free even under 'extreme edits' is that it never applies a single global remapping curve to the whole image (which is what produces unsharp-mask-style halos); instead each output Laplacian-pyramid coefficient is computed from ITS OWN locally-remapped copy of the image, re-built into a fresh local Laplacian pyramid, and only the one coefficient at that pixel/scale is kept — so no single physical 'ring' region around an edge is ever remapped identically to the region it borders, unlike unsharp masking which shares one global operator across the whole image.
descriptions.source: Aubry et al. 2014 slides: 'Unsharp Mask, not edge-aware [sad face] / Halo' vs 'Local Laplacian Filter, edge-aware [smile] [Paris et al. 2011] / No halos or gradient inversion / Even for extreme edits'; mechanism described via Algorithm 1 in Paris et al. 2011 ('for each coefficient (x0,y0,l0), we generate an intermediate image I~ by applying a point-wise monotonic remapping function... We compute the pyramid for the intermediate image and copy the corresponding coefficient to the output').

descriptions.claim: Aubry et al. 2014 explicitly name the two artifact classes: 'gradient reversals' (sign of the local gradient flips relative to the input) and 'halos' — and state that local Laplacian filters (LLF) are structurally resistant to gradient reversal while the bilateral filter is not.
descriptions.detail: Exact quote (Sec 2.2, Fig. 5 caption): 'These spurious variations are a source of halos and gradient reversals in applications such as tone mapping... both versions of the bilateral filter can generate reverse gradients on thin features (e.g., on the window frame) while the local Laplacian filters are not prone to this problem.' The mechanism given: the bilateral filter's per-pixel normalization (1/W_p) causes it to 'return an estimate based on limited data' near edges, over-sharpening them and injecting spurious content into the detail layer; the (unnormalized) multiscale LLF avoids this because it never normalizes and blends contributions from a full Gaussian-pyramid neighborhood at every scale rather than only the finest scale.
descriptions.source: Aubry, Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, Durand, 'Fast Local Laplacian Filters: Theory and Applications', ACM TOG 2014, Section 2.2 and Figure 5 caption (imagine.enpc.fr/~aubrym/projects/llf/texts/2014-fast-laplacian-filter.pdf)

descriptions.claim: Paris 2011 gives the exact origin of 'rim halos' as a naive-implementation failure mode: directly truncating/clipping Laplacian-pyramid coefficients (rather than doing point-wise remapping of the full-res signal per Gaussian-pyramid reference value) distorts the shape of the pyramid coefficients near edges, producing 'thin but unsightly rim halos' — this is the Li et al. 2005 approach they improve on.
descriptions.detail: Exact quote (Sec 3.2): 'A more sophisticated approach is to remark that edges correspond to large pyramid coefficients and selectively reduce these large values... the output signal is somewhat deformed near edges. Such deformations typically affect only a small band of pixels, manifested in images as thin but unsightly rim halos.' Mechanism: clipping Laplacian coefficients directly (I~ = min(max(I, g0-σr), g0+σr) then re-pyramiding) changes the WIDTH of the coefficient lobes at the edge (widens them vs. the true signal), which is the geometric root cause of the rim halo — visualized in their Figure 3 (b,c): 'the truncated coefficients form wider lobes whereas [Local Laplacian Filtering] produces profiles nearly identical to the input.'
descriptions.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz 2011, Section 3.2 ("Pyramid-based Range Compression") and Figure 3/4 captions, citing Li, Sharan, Adelson 2005

test_designs.claim: The 2011 paper's evaluation of artifact-freeness is entirely qualitative/comparative -- side-by-side figures against Li et al. 2005 (wavelets) and Farbman et al. 2008 (edge-preserving decomposition) on the same HDR image at matched detail levels, plus a bilateral-filter over-sharpening comparison and an edge-aware-wavelet aliasing comparison -- with no numeric halo metric.
test_designs.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, SIGGRAPH 2011, Fig. 15 caption: 'The wavelet-based method by Li et al. [2005] is best suited for neutral renditions and generates halos when one increases the level of detail... Farbman et al. [2008] ... halos and edge artifacts sometimes appear for a larger increase, as in this image for instance; see the edge of the white square on the blue book cover and the edge of the open book'; also Fig. 11 (comparison to Fattal 2009 edge-aware wavelets, extreme contrast near a light bulb) and Fig. 12 (bilateral filter over-sharpening, lamp-shade image)
test_designs.detail: Fig 16/17 stress test: full alpha in {0.25,0.5,2,4} x sigma_r in {0.1,0.2,0.4} grid and a combined 'HDR look' stress test (alpha=0.25, beta=0) are used only as qualitative demonstrations ('the results are dominated by local contrast ... but without the unsightly halos'), not scored numerically. The paper states these are best seen in supplemental material.

test_designs.claim: Hessel & Morel (2018) propose a rigorous four-artifact taxonomy for base/detail decomposition filters -- luminance halo, staircasing (gradient reversal), compartmentalization, and contrast halo -- each with an exact synthetic test-pattern and quantitative formula; 'compartmentalization' is the named literature term that matches the user's 'pooling' artifact (small bright/dark regions inside a differently-toned area being locally over/under-adjusted), and this paper is the single most directly reusable source for building a synthetic test suite.
test_designs.detail: Luminance halo measure: H(u0,u1) = sum_{x in Omega} 1_left(x)*[(u0(x)-u1(x))^+]^2 + sum_{x in Omega} 1_right(x)*[(u1(x)-u0(x))^+]^2 (Eq.12), where 1_left/1_right are indicator functions covering 40-pixel-wide rectangles flanking a vertical step edge, on a 6-panel composite test pattern with edge heights {0.3,0.4,0.5,0.6,0.7,0.8} stacked into horizontal bands. Staircase/gradient-reversal measure: S(u0,u1) = sum 1_left(x)*[(u1(x)-u0(x))^+]^2 + sum 1_right(x)*[(u0(x)-u1(x))^+]^2 (Eq.13) -- note the sign flip vs H: staircasing is measured where the filtered image OVERSHOOTS beyond the input on the wrong side, using a 6-panel pattern of a step edge pre-blurred with Gaussian sigma={0.7,1.4,2.8,5.6,11.2,22.4}. Compartmentalization measure: P(u0,u1) = var{(u0-u1)*1_inter} (Eq.14), computed on a test pattern of bright squares/rectangles of varying area on a dark background, with 1_inter masking out a 2-pixel band at every shape's inner/outer border (to exclude edge effects) so the measure isolates pure interior/interstice level-shift, i.e. exactly the 'small bright region gets a different net adjustment than its surround' pooling effect -- 'The artifact consists in an intensity shift of the detail in constant regions; its magnitude depends on the area of the region. The smaller the region, the stronger the artifact.' Contrast halo measure: C(u0,u1) = max(1, sigma_int^2/sigma_ext^2) - 1 (Eq.15), ratio of detail-layer variance inside a textured/noisy rectangle (sigma_int^2) vs. just outside its border (sigma_ext^2), on a texture-surrounded-by-contrasted-edges test pattern -- captures failure to smooth texture near strong edges (guided filter's worst artifact per this paper).
test_designs.source: Hessel, Morel, 'Quantitative Evaluation of Base and Detail Decomposition Filters Based on their Artifacts' (arXiv:1808.09411, 2018), Section IV 'The Main Artifacts and Their Test Patterns', Eq. 12-15

test_designs.claim: This project's own prior internal research (2026-05-31) already built a faithful, MIT-license-informed clean-room reimplementation of both Paris 2011 (exact) and Aubry 2014 (fast/discretized) local Laplacian filters, verified it against an identity test (max error 8.9e-16) and an edge-preservation test, and MEASURED halo severity using a specific, reusable metric: 'edge-band overshoot/undershoot amplitude' of a high-contrast step edge with fine micro-texture on both plateaus, pushed through the full DR-compression operator, measured against the two plateaus' globally-compressed target levels.
test_designs.source: Internal repo doc: docs/archive/research/v10c-local-laplacian-base-deferred.md, Sections 1-2 (measurement table: guided r=8 baseline 0.0685, fast LLF sigma_r-spacing 0.0601 (marginal win), fast LLF sigma_r/2 0.0881 (loses), fast LLF sigma_r/4 0.0978 (loses), exact LLF (naive O(N^2)) 0.1120 (worst)).
test_designs.detail: CRITICAL prior finding directly relevant to calibrating the user's own operator: this project already empirically found that REFINING the LLF discretization (finer gamma grid, closer to the exact/theoretical method) makes edge-band overshoot WORSE, not better, when LLF is used to build a base layer that is subsequently compressed by an EXTERNAL fixed tone law and recombined with unity-gain detail (the architecture the user describes: 'ABSOLUTE calibrated tone curve added to the Gaussian pyramid RESIDUAL'). The doc's own root-cause analysis (Section 3): LLF's halo-free guarantee is a property of Paris 2011's INTEGRATED construction where the SAME point-wise nonlinearity both extracts the base AND compresses tone in one pass; it does NOT transfer to an architecture that severs base-extraction from tone-compression (external law applied post-hoc to an LLF-extracted base). Given the user's stated architecture (identity detail arm, edge arm slope 1+delta'(gamma), absolute curve added to the residual) sounds like exactly this severed architecture, this prior finding predicts their observed H1/H2/H3 artifacts are structurally expected, not a bug -- and that simply refining scale/parameters within the same architecture will not fix it; an architectural change (recoupling remap-and-compress, or switching to a fundamentally different base producer such as the guided filter this project ended up shipping) is what worked here.

test_designs.claim: Aubry 2014 demonstrates a concrete, numerically-specified halo-comparison baseline: sigma_r set to 10% of a step edge's amplitude produces visibly different halo/gradient-reversal behavior between the (normalized) bilateral filter and their unnormalized variant on both a soft edge and a small isolated detail of amplitude = 10% of the edge amplitude — giving exact relative-amplitude numbers for a minimal reproducible halo test case.
test_designs.detail: Exact quote (Fig. 5 caption): 'The top row shows a soft edge (left). The standard filter over-sharpens the edge (center) and introduces non-negligible content in the detail layer (right)... The bottom row shows an isolated small detail (left)... whose height is 10% of the edge amplitude. The standard bilateral filter smooths such details more aggressively than the unnormalized version... Both filters were set with σr equal to 10% of the edge amplitude.' This is a directly transferable synthetic test-case recipe: a step edge plus an isolated small-amplitude bump (10% of edge height), sigma_r pinned at 10% of edge amplitude — useful as a calibration harness for a numpy implementation's own halo/pooling regression tests.

test_designs.claim: Hessel & Morel's cross-filter calibration methodology — fixing all compared filters to the SAME output PSNR relative to input before comparing artifact scores — is a transferable protocol for isolating 'does this filter's mitigation reduce artifacts at equal strength of effect' rather than conflating artifact reduction with weaker overall processing.
test_designs.detail: Exact quote and formula: to avoid penalizing filters that merely produce smaller detail layers, they equalize PSNR(u0-u1) = -10×log10((1/√N)·||u0-u1||_2), targeting PSNR = 16.23 dB across all filters ('a decent amount of detail for all filters') before measuring H, S, P, C. This matters directly for the described pipeline: any mitigation for exposure-halos/pooling must be evaluated at matched adjustment STRENGTH (e.g. matched mean |ΔEV| or matched detail-layer energy), not by comparing a strong vs. weak setting, or the halo suppression will be confounded with simply doing less.
test_designs.source: Hessel & Morel, arXiv:1808.09411, Section V ('Setting the Parameters of Each Filter'), Equation 16

test_designs.claim: The Hessel/Morel taxonomy implies a specific, actionable test-pattern design pattern directly reusable for a numpy pipeline's regression suite: pair each artifact class with a MINIMAL synthetic pattern (flat regions + single step edge at swept amplitudes for halo/staircase; isolated bright rectangles of swept AREA on dark background for compartmentalization/pooling; noise texture bounded by contrasted edges of swept width for contrast-halo) rather than testing on full photographs, because isolating one artifact per pattern prevents cross-contamination between artifact types in a single score.
test_designs.detail: Direct methodological statement: 'First, we need to test different edge heights. Yet we also need to dispose of patterns with only one edge. Indeed, this ensures that the deformation caused by the filter (the artifacts!) does not come from another nearby edge.' They use SIX swept-parameter variants per pattern stacked into one composite image for batch measurement (edge heights {0.3,0.4,0.5,0.6,0.7,0.8}; blur widths σ={0.7,1.4,2.8,5.6,11.2,22.4}), and use rectangular masks sized to the filter's own spatial support (40px = the tested filters' sigma) placed adjacent to the edge to localize where each measurement is taken — directly transferable to designing a synthetic 'bright strip next to dark curtain' compartmentalization/pooling regression fixture with swept strip widths.
test_designs.source: Hessel & Morel, arXiv:1808.09411, Section IV-A(a) and IV-B(a) (test-pattern construction)

checked.claim: The 2014 Fast LLF paper's analytical two-level derivation of the tone-mapping formula explicitly ASSUMES the coarsest pyramid level (residual) is left completely unmodified: L1[O] = L1[I].
checked.source: Aubry, Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, Durand, 'Fast Local Laplacian Filters,' TOG 2014, Sec 2.1.1, p.4: 'We assume for now that the residual remains unprocessed, that is L1[O] = L1[I]'
checked.detail: This assumption underlies the derivation of Eq. 8-10, which shows the two-level LLF is equivalent to Op = Ip + sum_q Gsigma_p(q-p) f(Iq-Ip)(Iq-Ip) (Eq. 10), a local-average/bilateral-like formula obtained 'by upsampling the unmodified residual, adding it to both sides, and expanding the convolution.' The paper does not claim this is how the full multiscale filter treats the true top pyramid level in general (only used for the simplified 2-level analytical case), but it establishes the residual-passthrough convention used to derive the paper's core theory.

verified.corrected_source: Aubry, M., Paris, S., Hasinoff, S. W., Kautz, J., Durand, F. "Fast Local Laplacian Filters: Theory and Applications." ACM Transactions on Graphics (TOG), Vol. 33, No. 5, Article 167, 2014. Section 2.1.1 "Single-scale filter" (within Section 2.1 "Analysis of local Laplacian filters"), page 4, right column. Verbatim primary-source PDF (https://imagine.enpc.fr/~aubrym/projects/llf/texts/2014-fast-laplacian-filter.pdf) reads: "We first consider a two-level pyramid, that is, we seek to compute the levels L0[O] and L1[O] of the Laplacian pyramid of the output image O. We assume for now that the residual remains unprocessed, that is L1[O] = L1[I]." This is immediately followed by Eq. 8 giving L0[O](p).
verified.note:
CONFIRMED verbatim and located precisely. Fetched the primary-source PDF directly from the first author's project page (Mathieu Aubry, INRIA/ENS host: imagine.enpc.fr) and read the actual page image. The sentence "We assume for now that the residual remains unprocessed, that is L1[O] = L1[I]" appears exactly as quoted, at the start of Section 2.1.1 "Single-scale filter," which is a subsection of Section 2.1 "Analysis of local Laplacian filters," on page 4 (page number visibly printed in the header "4 • M. Aubry et al."). No discrepancy in wording, section number, or page number vs. the claim.

One nuance for context, not a refutation: this is explicitly a temporary/local simplifying assumption used only to derive the two-level (single-scale) special case that links LLF to bilateral filtering (Eq. 8-10). The paper's very next subsection, 2.1.2 "Multi-scale filter" (also page 5), drops the two-level restriction and gives the general L_ℓ[O] formula (Eq. 12-13) for arbitrary pyramid depth — so the "unmodified residual" is a derivational scaffold for the theoretical analysis, not a description of how the shipped multi-scale/production LLF algorithm treats the coarsest level in general. The claim as worded ("assumes... for now") already reflects this correctly by including "for now," so it is not misleading — just flagging the scope for anyone using this citation to argue about general LLF residual behavior beyond the two-level derivation.

checked.claim: In the fast/discretized approximation, the 2014 paper builds a small set of precomputed Laplacian pyramids at regularly-sampled reference intensities {gamma_j} and reconstructs each output coefficient by LINEAR interpolation between the two bracketing pyramids -- this is the exact mechanism (and source) of intensity-discretization artifacts.
checked.source: Aubry, Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, Durand, TOG 2014, Sec 3 ('Efficient Local Laplacian Filtering'), algorithm steps (1)-(5), esp. step 4.ii-iii: 'Compute a and j such that g=(1-a)*gamma_j + a*gamma_{j+1}. ... Linearly interpolate the output coefficient from the precomputed pyramids: L_l[O](x,y) = (1-a)*L_l[r_j(I)](x,y) + a*L_l[r_{j+1}(I)](x,y)'
checked.detail: The paper frames the choice of sampling density as a signal-processing/Nyquist problem: 'if r is band-limited, using the sampling theorem, the optimal sampling is the Nyquist limit, i.e., half the smallest wavelength present in the signal ... what actually matters is the frequency content of x*f(x) ... F[x f(x)] is proportional to F[f]', i.e. if f (e.g. a Gaussian) is band-limited, r is too, justifying sparse sampling of g.

verified.corrected_source: Aubry, M., Paris, S., Hasinoff, S. W., Kautz, J., Durand, F. "Fast Local Laplacian Filters: Theory and Applications." ACM Transactions on Graphics, Vol. 33, No. 5, Article 167 (2014). Sec. 3 "EFFICIENT LOCAL LAPLACIAN FILTERING" (p.6), algorithm steps (1)-(5). Verified verbatim against the author's PDF (https://imagine.enpc.fr/~aubrym/projects/llf/data/Aubry_fastLLF_siggraph14.pdf), which reads exactly: "(4) For each pixel coefficient (l,x,y): i. Get the corresponding coefficient g in the Gaussian pyramid. ii. Compute a and j such that g=(1-a)gamma_j + a*gamma_{j+1}. iii. Linearly interpolate the output coefficient from the precomputed pyramids: L_l[O](x,y) = (1-a) L_l[r_j(I)](x,y) + a L_l[r_{j+1}(I)](x,y)."
verified.note:
The quoted algorithm text and citation (author, venue, section, step numbers) are verbatim-correct -- confirmed by direct primary-source PDF retrieval, not a paraphrase from secondary sources. The mechanism description (precompute pyramids at {gamma_j}, reconstruct each output coefficient by linear interpolation between the two bracketing pyramids) is exactly what Sec. 3 describes and is the core of the paper's 50x acceleration.

One overreach to flag: the claim's framing that this is "the exact mechanism (and source) of intensity-discretization artifacts" is NOT how the 2014 paper characterizes its own scheme. Aubry et al. explicitly claim the opposite in the same section/page: sampling every standard deviation "produces accurate approximations above 30dB and the differences are invisible in practice," and that their scheme is "guaranteed to approximate the original scheme" (Paris et al. 2011's exact/unaccelerated formulation). They do NOT use the terms "discretization artifacts" or "banding" anywhere in the paper. The characterization of linear pyramid-interpolation-over-discretized-gamma-levels as a source of visible discretization/banding artifacts is a critique that appears in LATER follow-up literature (e.g. arXiv:2206.04681 "Gaussian Fourier Pyramid for Local Laplacian Filter," and the NeurIPS "Lookup Table meets Local Laplacian Filter" paper, arXiv:2310.17190), which motivate their own improvements (Fourier-series reconstruction, learned LUTs) by pointing out that plain linear interpolation between discretely-sampled pyramids is a lossy approximation. So: the quoted text/algorithm/citation is dead-on and directly verified against the primary source; the artifact-causation framing wrapped around it is an inference/claim from later literature being retrofitted onto the 2014 paper, not something the 2014 paper itself asserts. If precision matters, the corrected framing is: "this IS the mechanism later literature identifies as the source of intensity-discretization/banding artifacts in fast LLF; the 2014 paper itself claims (not conclusively proven, no formal error bound beyond an empirical >30dB PSNR claim) that the approximation error from this scheme is visually negligible."

checked.claim: The 2014 paper's recommended intensity-sampling density is one sample per standard deviation of the range kernel (sigma), which it reports as producing accurate approximations above 30 dB PSNR against the exact (non-discretized) LLF output, and running at about 350 ms/megapixel on a single CPU core (about 50x faster than the original 2011 heuristic at matched accuracy).
checked.source: Aubry, Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, Durand, TOG 2014, Sec 3, and Fig. 8 caption: 'In practice, a Gaussian function G_sigma is used for f, e.g., for the detail enhancement, we recommend sampling the intensity range every standard deviation sigma'; 'it produces accurate approximations above 30 dB and the differences are invisible in practice ... when sampling every standard deviation, it runs at interactive rates, about 350 ms per megapixel'; Fig 8: 'The square marks on the curve of our method indicate the one-sample-per-standard-deviation sampling rate'
checked.detail: Fig. 8 plots running time (s, log scale) vs PSNR (dB) for both Paris et al.'s original heuristic (varying sub-pyramid depth) and this paper's method (varying intensity sampling rate), across three parameter regimes (large detail increase, moderate increase, moderate decrease) -- this IS the paper's quantitative accuracy/error evaluation methodology (an accuracy-vs-speed Pareto curve, not a fixed metric formula).

verified.corrected_source: Aubry, M., Paris, S., Hasinoff, S. W., Kautz, J., Durand, F. "Fast Local Laplacian Filters: Theory and Applications." ACM Transactions on Graphics, Vol. 33, No. 5, Article 167, 2014. Section 3 ("Efficient Local Laplacian Filtering"), body text, and the Figure 8 caption. Full paper PDF verified at https://imagine.enpc.fr/~aubrym/projects/llf/texts/2014-fast-laplacian-filter.pdf

checked.claim: The shadows/highlights tone curve is a piecewise quadratic-Bezier blend in the *detail residual* c=x-g (not an absolute value), clamped to a fixed ±2*sigma transition window per gamma-reference level g, with the shadows/highlights gain applied ONLY beyond that window: `if (c > 2*sigma) val = g + sigma + shadows*(c-sigma); else if (c < -2*sigma) val = g - sigma + highlights*(c+sigma);` — this is the exact mechanism the user should compare their 'ABSOLUTE calibrated tone curve added to residual' design against, since darktable deliberately keeps the shift a function of the local detail coefficient c, not an absolute offset.
checked.source: src/common/locallaplacian.c, curve_scalar() lines 313-343

verified.note:
MOSTLY CONFIRMED but the claim MISREPRESENTS the mechanism by omission, so it should not be forwarded as-is. Verified true: (1) c = x-g is the detail residual, computed in absolute intensity, exactly as claimed; (2) the ±2σ transition window is real and is exactly as described — the branch split points are literally `c > 2*sigma` and `c < -2*sigma`; (3) the two boundary-branch lines the claim quotes as "the exact mechanism" (`if (c > 2*sigma) val = g + sigma + shadows*(c-sigma); else if (c < -2*sigma) val = g - sigma + highlights*(c+sigma);`) are verbatim-correct excerpts from lines 324-325; (4) citation (file + exact line range 313-343) is accurate and not fabricated.

FALSE/MISLEADING part: the claim states "shadows/highlights gain applied ONLY beyond that window" — this is refuted by the code itself. `shadows` and `highlights` also appear inside the two middle branches (`c>0.0f` "shadow contrast" and the `else` "highlight contrast" branch, lines 326-339), where they scale the quadratic-Bezier blend term (`t2*(sigma + sigma*shadows)` and `t2*(-sigma-sigma*highlights)`). So the gain is progressively blended in starting at c=0 (t=0, no gain contribution) and ramping to full linear gain at |c|=2σ, continuing linearly beyond — it is not a hard on/off gate at the window boundary. The claim's own pasted "exact mechanism" snippet is an incomplete quotation: it silently drops half the function (the two Bezier blend branches) and the final clarity term (line 341, `val += clarity * c * dt_fast_expf(...)`), then asserts the remainder is "the exact mechanism," which overstates what was actually quoted. Net: the source is real and correctly located, but the claim built on top of it (gain applied "ONLY beyond" the window) is factually wrong per the same source — a downstream reader relying on this claim to design a comparison would get the boundary behavior wrong (they'd expect a discontinuous gate rather than the smooth Bezier ramp that darktable actually uses precisely to avoid the halo/banding a hard gate would cause — which is the whole point of the "blend in via quadratic bezier" comment on line 323). Corrected characterization: shadows/highlights gain is smoothly ramped in via a quadratic Bezier starting at c=0, reaching full linear-gain slope at |c|=2σ, and continuing as a pure linear extension beyond ±2σ — not "applied only beyond" the window.

checked.claim: The 'clarity'/local-contrast boost term is a Gaussian-windowed function of the residual, `val += clarity * c * dt_fast_expf(-c*c/(2.0f*sigma*sigma/3.0f))`, i.e. an envelope of standard deviation sigma*sqrt(1/3) centered on c=0 — this Gaussian falloff (not a hard clamp) is darktable's specific mechanism for confining local-contrast boost to near-zero-detail (flat/near-edge) coefficients and letting it decay smoothly for large |c|, which is the direct analogue of the user's 'detail arm identity |d|<=sigma_r' design but implemented as a smooth exponential taper rather than a hard threshold.
checked.source: src/common/locallaplacian.c, curve_scalar() line 341

verified.corrected_source: darktable src/common/locallaplacian.c, function curve_scalar(), line 341 (current master): `val += clarity * c * dt_fast_expf(-c*c/(2.0f*sigma*sigma/3.0f));` — the code quote and line number in the claim are verbatim-correct (verified by direct fetch of the raw file). Primary source for the design rationale (not cited in the claim, but load-bearing): hanatos, "local laplacian pyramids" blog post, https://jo.dreggn.org/blog/laplacian/post.html (mirrored at https://www.darktable.org/2017/11/local-laplacian-pyramids/).
verified.note: Two-part verdict. (1) VERBATIM/MATH — CONFIRMED: the exact code string and line number (341) are correct as cited, and the algebra is right (matching exp(-c^2/(2*sigma_env^2)) to the code's exp(-c^2/(2*sigma^2/3)) gives sigma_env = sigma*sqrt(1/3)). (2) CAUSAL/DESIGN-INTENT CLAIM — REFUTED: the claim asserts this Gaussian falloff is darktable's 'specific mechanism for confining local-contrast boost to near-zero-detail (flat/near-edge) coefficients' to prevent halos, analogous to a hard-threshold detail-identity design. The primary source (the algorithm author's own writeup, which I fetched and grepped in raw text) gives a different, explicit rationale: the Gaussian-derivative shape (vs. the original paper's kinked piecewise curve) was chosen purely so the curve family is band-limited, which is required for the fast local-Laplacian-pyramid approximation (Paris et al.) to be valid without producing aliasing artifacts. That post never uses the word 'sigma' and mentions 'halo' exactly once, in an unrelated remark about exposure-fusion tone mapping — never connecting it to this term. So this is a numerical/signal-processing correctness constraint (avoid aliasing in a fixed small number of pyramid levels), not a stated halo-suppression/edge-detection design choice. Additionally, curve_scalar is not simply 'a Gaussian-windowed function of the residual' — it's a 3-region piecewise curve (linear tails beyond |c|>2*sigma, quadratic-Bezier shadow/highlight-contrast blends for 0<|c|<=2*sigma) onto which the Gaussian-windowed clarity term is additively superimposed for all c. The claim's drawn analogy to a hard-threshold |d|<=sigma_r 'detail arm identity' is the questioner's own inference, unsupported by (and in framing, contradicted by) the primary source's stated reasoning.

checked.claim: darktable discretizes the continuous remapping function into num_gamma=6 fixed reference brightness levels sampled at gamma[k]=(k+0.5)/6 for k=0..5 (i.e. 0.083, 0.25, 0.417, 0.583, 0.75, 0.917), builds a full Laplacian pyramid for EACH of the 6 curve-remapped copies of the image, and reconstructs each output pixel by linearly interpolating the Laplacian coefficient between the two bracketing gamma levels: `a = CLAMPS((v-gamma[lo])/(gamma[hi]-gamma[lo]),0,1); output[l] += l0*(1-a) + l1*a`. Coarser num_gamma sampling (or non-band-limited curves) is the developers' explicitly named cause of aliasing/banding artifacts.
checked.source: src/common/locallaplacian.c lines 32, 444-445, 467-474, 543-559

verified.corrected_source: Primary source for the code mechanics: github.com/darktable-org/darktable, src/common/locallaplacian.c (master branch). Verified by direct fetch of raw.githubusercontent.com/darktable-org/darktable/master/src/common/locallaplacian.c on 2026-07-07. Exact line numbers on current master: line 32 `#define num_gamma 6`; lines 444-445 `float gamma[num_gamma] = {0.0f}; for(int k=0;k<num_gamma;k++) gamma[k] = (k+.5f)/(float)num_gamma;`; lines 467-474 the per-gamma pyramid-build loop (`apply_curve` + `gauss_reduce` per k); lines 543-559 the reconstruction loop with `v`, `hi`/`lo` bracket search, `a = CLAMPS((v - gamma[lo])/(gamma[hi]-gamma[lo]), 0.0f, 1.0f)`, `l0`/`l1` via `ll_laplacian`, and `output[l][...] += l0*(1.0f-a) + l1*a`. Also cross-confirmed in the OpenCL kernel locallaplacian.cl, which hardcodes `const float num_gamma = 6.0f; // this sucks, have to hardcode for the lack of arrays` (per PR #1403, github.com/darktable-org/darktable/pull/1403). The "non-band-limited curve causes aliasing" statement is from the darktable dev blog, not the .c file: "local laplacian pyramids", darktable.org, posted 2017-11-21 by jo, https://www.darktable.org/2017/11/local-laplacian-pyramids/ (near-identical draft at https://jo.dreggn.org/blog/laplacian/post.html), quoting section 3 of Aubry/Paris/Hasinoff/Kautz/Durand, "Fast Local Laplacian Filters: Theory and Applications" (ACM TOG 2014) on the band-limited requirement.
verified.note:
Parts 1-3 of the claim (num_gamma=6, gamma[k]=(k+.5)/6 giving 0.083/0.25/0.417/0.583/0.75/0.917, per-gamma pyramid construction, and the exact interpolation formula/variable names a/l0/l1) are confirmed essentially verbatim against the live darktable master source, including the cited line numbers, which line up almost exactly with the current file. This is as close to a bit-exact code citation as adversarial verification gets.

Part 4 is where the claim overreaches. The "non-band-limited curve -> random aliasing" causal statement IS a real, direct, near-verbatim developer quote -- but it comes from the darktable.org dev blog post "local laplacian pyramids" (Nov 2017), not from locallaplacian.c, and not from the specific cited line ranges. The blog's own words: "don't use this curve at home. it will produce random aliasing when used with the fast local laplacian code" -- referring to using a curve with sharp kinks (non-band-limited) for the per-pixel remapping function, which is why darktable's curve_scalar() uses a smooth Gaussian-derivative/quadratic-Bezier blend instead. Neither the blog post nor the .c source file ever states or implies that a coarser num_gamma value (fewer than 6 levels) causes aliasing or banding -- num_gamma's value (n, "a small, fixed number") is discussed only in terms of speed/memory tradeoff (full pyramids at every level needed for correctness, not "n could be higher/lower to trade off artifact risk"). So the claim's final sentence conflates two genuinely-sourced but textually separate ideas (non-band-limited curve shape -> aliasing, which is real and quotable; and num_gamma coarseness -> aliasing/banding, which is not attested anywhere I could find in the source file, the two blog posts, the PR #1403 discussion, or GitHub issue search). Recommend citing the blog post for the band-limiting/aliasing causal claim and dropping or hedging the "coarser num_gamma sampling" causation, since it is not "explicitly named" by the developers in any primary source found.

checked.claim: The darktable.org 2017 blog post by Hanika states outright that a non-band-limited (kinked) remapping curve 'will produce random aliasing when used with the fast local laplacian code' and that the whole point of using a derivative-of-Gaussian S-curve blended via quadratic Bezier (rather than e.g. a hard clip) is to keep the curve band-limited so num_gamma=6 discretization stays accurate — this is the developer's stated causal mechanism for banding-type artifacts when users push settings to extremes.

verified.corrected_source: https://www.darktable.org/2017/11/local-laplacian-pyramids/ — "local laplacian pyramids", darktable blog, posted Tue 21 Nov 2017, author "jo" (Johannes Hanika). URL is correct and the post is real, but the claim over-states/conflates what it says.
verified.note:
PARTIALLY CONFIRMED / PARTIALLY REFUTED. I fetched the live page directly (both via WebFetch and a raw curl + tag-strip, full text reproduced) and can quote it exactly.

What IS true, verbatim from the post:
- On the *original paper's* [1] mapping curve (Paris et al., the "fast local laplacian filters" ref [2] motivates a small fixed n only "if r is band-limited"): "now if you look at the left image (which is taken from fig. 6 in [1]), it has clear kinks where the center part joints the straight lines. not band limited at all. don't use this curve at home. it will produce random aliasing when used with the fast local laplacian code."
- Immediately after: "darktable uses what is shown on the right instead: the contrast-s curve in the center part is modelled by a derivative of gaussian (with infinite support), which is added to the straight lines on either side, which are blended over using a quadratic bezier curve."

So the core causal claim — kinked/non-band-limited curve -> random aliasing with the fast local laplacian code; darktable's DoG+quadratic-Bezier S-curve exists to keep the curve band-limited — is accurately sourced to this exact post and is essentially correctly paraphrased. However the claim as given misattributes whose curve is being warned about: the post is warning about the curve from the ORIGINAL paper's figure (a curve the post explicitly says NOT to use — "don't use this curve at home"), not about a kinked curve darktable itself ships or that users can dial in. It's not framed as something that happens when "users push settings to extremes" — no such framing exists anywhere in the post.

What is NOT in the source (fabricated/conflated):
- The phrase/term "num_gamma" or "num_gamma=6" never appears anywhere in this blog post (grep of the full extracted text returns zero hits). num_gamma IS a real variable in darktable's C source (src/common/locallaplacian.c), controlling how many gamma-remapped pyramid copies are built — but that is source code, not this blog post, and the post never ties band-limiting to a specific discretization count.
- The word "banding" never appears in the post; the post's own word is "aliasing" (and separately, in an unrelated paragraph, "structured artifacts for noisy input" when discussing chroma noise — not the same claim).
- There is no statement in the post connecting curve kinks to what happens "when users push settings to extremes" — that framing is not in the source; it appears to be an inference/embellishment added on top of the real Hanika text.

Net: the URL and core causal mechanism (kink -> aliasing, DoG/Bezier -> band-limited-ness) are real and correctly quoted from Hanika's 2017 post, but the claim smuggles in a specific figure (num_gamma=6) and a specific word (banding) and a framing (users pushing sliders to extremes) that are not supported by this source. Recommend citing only the two verbatim sentences quoted above and dropping the num_gamma/banding/extremes framing, or sourcing those separately from src/common/locallaplacian.c if needed.
