Core claim is substantially TRUE and well-sourced, but contains one real inaccuracy and one overstatement, so I'm marking confirmed=false for the claim AS WORDED (it needs correction before it can be cited as-is):

(1) INACCURACY — mechanism misattribution: the claim says out-of-ROI PADDING pixels are "looked up via trilinear interpolation... instead of simple edge-replication." False as stated: the padding fill in ll_pad_input (lines 230-297) uses NEAREST-NEIGHBOR lookup into b->pad0 — the code literally has a comment `/* TODO: linear interpolation?*/` at line 253 showing it wasn't even upgraded to bilinear. The genuine trilinear interpolation (weighted blend of b->output[pl0] and b->output[pl1], each themselves bilinearly sampled — hence "trilinear") lives in a DIFFERENT code block (lines 476-534, specifically 497-530) that resamples the coarsest Gaussian/Laplacian pyramid level to match the preview pipeline's mip pyramid, not the boundary-padding pixels per se. Both mechanisms exist and are both part of mode==2, both real, both dead — but the claim conflates which one does the trilinear interpolation.

(2) OVERSTATEMENT — issue status: claim says #3418/#4080 remain "open/'not possible, won't fix.'" Both are actually CLOSED (verified via `gh issue view --json state,stateReason`, state=CLOSED, stateReason=COMPLETED), closed automatically by a stale-issue bot after inactivity — not kept open, and not explicitly closed by a maintainer decision either. The "Not possible. Won't fix." quote is 100% verbatim and correctly attributed to aurelienpierre on #4080, but it's a comment mid-thread, not the closing action.

Everything else checks out precisely: the struct exists, the header comment is verbatim, the mode enum values are verbatim, the mode==1/mode==2 branches exist at essentially the cited line ranges, the bilat.c call site passes a literal 0 (confirmed the ONLY call to local_laplacian() in the whole repo via GitHub code search — total_count:2, matching only locallaplacian.h's declaration and bilat.c's call), and the "referenced ONLY inside locallaplacian.c/.h" grep claim is confirmed by GitHub's code-search API (total_count:2). The "dead infrastructure" characterization is well-supported: no other module (filmic, shadows-highlights, contrast equalizer, etc.) ever passes a non-NULL boundary struct.

Recommended correction if using this claim: soften "trilinear interpolation... instead of simple edge-replication" to specifically describe the coarsest-pyramid-level resample (lines 476-534/497-530) as the trilinear mechanism, and separately/correctly describe the padding-pixel fill (lines 230-297) as nearest-neighbor-into-preview (with an unimplemented TODO for linear interpolation) replacing edge-replication only for its own out-of-buffer fallback case. Also change "remaining open" to "both now closed, auto-closed by stale-bot inactivity, no maintainer fix ever landed."

checked.claim: Guided filter halos are explained via the epsilon regularization threshold that classifies a local window as 'flat patch' vs 'high variance' patch; halos occur where this classification is ambiguous, and the paper states this explicitly as an unresolved limitation.
checked.detail: He/Sun/Tang define the guided filter output as q_i = a_k*I_i + b_k in window omega_k, solved by minimizing E(a_k,b_k) = sum_{i in omega_k}[(a_k*I_i+b_k-p_i)^2 + eps*a_k^2], giving a_k = (cov)/(sigma_k^2+eps), b_k = mean_p - a_k*mean_I. epsilon acts exactly like bilateral sigma_r^2: 'The effect of eps in the guided filter is similar with the range variance sigma_r^2 in the bilateral filter. Both parameters determine what is an edge/a high variance patch that should be preserved.' Case eps=0 gives a_k=1,b_k=0 (identity); patches with variance << eps are smoothed (flat patch, a_k->0), patches with variance >> eps are preserved (a_k->1). Critically, in the Discussion (Sec 5) the authors state: 'As a locally based operator, the guided filter is not directly applicable for sparse inputs like strokes. It also shares a common limitation of other explicit filter - it may have halos near some edges. In fact, it is ambiguous for a low-level and local operator to determine which edge should be smoothed and which should be preserved. Unsuitably smoothing an edge will result in halos near it.' Concrete parameter pairs used in their figures: r=16, eps=0.1^2 (detail enhancement, Fig.6); r=15, eps=0.12^2 (HDR compression, Fig.7) -- always with eps expressed as a squared intensity value in [0,1] units, directly comparable to bilateral sigma_r.
checked.source: He, Sun, Tang, 'Guided Image Filtering' (ECCV 2010 preprint PDF, people.csail.mit.edu/kaiming/publications/eccv10guidedfilter.pdf), Sections 3.1-3.2 and Section 5 Discussion

verified.corrected_source: He, K., Sun, J., Tang, X. "Guided Image Filtering." ECCV 2010. Springer LNCS 6311, pp. 1–14. PDF verified at people.csail.mit.edu/kaiming/publications/eccv10guidedfilter.pdf (downloaded and full-text extracted directly, 14 pages). Relevant primary text: Section 3.2 "Edge-preserving Filtering" (p.5) defines Case 1 "Flat patch" (variance ≪ ε) and Case 2 "High variance" (variance ≫ ε), stating "the criterion of a 'flat patch' or a 'high variance' is given by the parameter ε. The patches with variance (σ²) much smaller than ε are smoothed, whereas those with variance much larger than ε are preserved." Section 5 is indeed titled "Discussion and Conclusion" (p.13) and states: "It also shares a common limitation of other explicit filter - it may have halos near some edges. In fact, it is ambiguous for a low-level and local operator to determine which edge should be smoothed and which should be preserved. Unsuitably smoothing an edge will result in halos near it."
verified.note: PARTIALLY CONFIRMED / MISLEADING — the citation itself is accurate (real paper, real sections, both quotes exist verbatim as described), but the CAUSAL CLAIM is not what the paper says. Refuted specifics: (1) The paper's Section 5 halo discussion does NOT explicitly invoke or reference the epsilon threshold or the flat-patch/high-variance classification from 3.2 at all — it gives a different, more general explanation: halos arise because it is ambiguous for ANY low-level local operator to know "which edge should be smoothed and which should be preserved" (a semantic/edge-significance ambiguity), not an ambiguity in the σ² vs ε numerical classification itself. (2) Section 3.2 discusses the flat/high-variance dichotomy purely as an intuitive explanation of the filter's edge-preserving behavior — it says nothing about this classification being a source of halos or being "ambiguous" in cases near the ε boundary. (3) The paper never uses the word "unresolved," and nowhere does it state that halo-causing ambiguity is specifically about borderline/ambiguous ε-threshold classification of a window as flat vs. high-variance. So the claim fabricates an explicit textual link between 3.2's mechanism and 5's limitation that the primary source does not make — it stitches together two real but separate passages into a causal claim the authors never assert. A correct characterization: the paper attributes halos to the general local-operator problem of not knowing which edges are semantically meant to be preserved vs. smoothed (edge significance ambiguity), and separately (without connecting the two) explains that ε acts as a variance threshold that determines the flat-vs-edge behavior of the local linear model. The two ARE mechanistically related in a sense obvious to someone versed in the math (windows whose variance is near ε get inconsistent a_k depending on exact local statistics, which can create halo-like inconsistency between overlapping windows per Eq. 7-8's averaging step) — but that inferential leap is the reader's/claimant's own reasoning, not something "the paper states explicitly," which is what the claim asserts.

checked.detail: Exact text: 'Durand and Dorsey [2002] describe a variant designed specifically to avoid halos along thin, high contrast features, while Bae et al. [2006] detect and fix reversed gradients when manipulating the detail layer. However, the former approach does not eliminate wider halos, while the latter requires solving a Poisson equation as a postprocess.' Also cites Mantiuk et al.'s multi-resolution gradient-domain framework as preventing halos 'by imposing constraints on contrast at multiple spatial frequencies' as an alternative mitigation family (gradient-domain rather than base/detail).

Exact sentence: "Durand and Dorsey [2002] describe a variant designed specifically to avoid halos along thin, high contrast features, while Bae et al. [2006] detect and fix reversed gradients when manipulating the detail layer. However, the former approach does not eliminate wider halos, while the latter requires solving a Poisson equation as a postprocess."
