Substance matches: (1) Durand & Dorsey 2002 variant targets/avoids halos specifically "along thin, high contrast features" — matches claim's "thin, high-contrast-feature halos (correctable)"; (2) paper explicitly states this approach "does not eliminate wider halos" — matches claim's "does NOT eliminate wide halos." Location matches: Section 2, the related-work paragraph on BLF halo-mitigation variants (Elad 2002, Choudhury/Tumblin 2003, Buades 2006, Durand/Dorsey 2002, Bae et al. 2006).

Caveats: (a) the paper does not use the claim's exact category labels "thin rim halos" and "wider halos from progressive coarsening" — those are the claim-writer's own framing/paraphrase, not the paper's terminology, though "progressive coarsening" as the general cause of halo artifacts IS discussed elsewhere on the same page (Section 1, re: BLF's limited progressive-coarsening ability) so the connection is reasonable, not fabricated; (b) minor wording drift "wide" vs paper's "wider" (comparative). Net: the core factual claim (Durand-Dorsey 2002 fix eliminates only thin/high-contrast halos, not wide/wider ones, per this exact paper/section) is directly and verifiably TRUE against primary-source text, not a hallucinated or misattributed citation.

checked.claim: The WLS smoothness-weight construction that avoids halos: the regularization term's spatially-varying weights a_x, a_y are typically set as a_x,p(g) = 1/(|dL/dx(p)|^alpha + eps), i.e. inversely proportional to the gradient magnitude of the LOG-luminance image raised to a small exponent alpha (paper uses alpha in [1.2, 2.0] and eps ~ 0.0001), which lets the smoothing degree scale independently at each pixel without trading off spatial vs range kernel size the way the bilateral filter must.
checked.detail: From the objective: minimize sum_p [(u_p-g_p)^2 + lambda(a_x,p(g)(du/dx)_p^2 + a_y,p(g)(du/dy)_p^2)], solved as the linear system (I + lambda*L_g) u = g where L_g = D_x^T A_x D_x + D_y^T A_y D_y. Because this is a single global optimization (not a local windowed average like the bilateral/guided filter), there is no local sigma_s vs sigma_r tradeoff -- lambda alone controls the overall degree of smoothing, and the per-pixel weight a(g) alone controls where edges are protected, decoupling 'how much to smooth' from 'where to stop.'
checked.source: Farbman, Fattal, Lischinski, Szeliski, 'Edge-Preserving Decompositions...' (SIGGRAPH 2008), Section 3 ('Edge-Preserving Smoothing via WLS'), Eq. 3-5

verified.corrected_source: Farbman, Z., Fattal, R., Lischinski, D., and Szeliski, R., "Edge-Preserving Decompositions for Multi-Scale Tone and Detail Manipulation," ACM Transactions on Graphics (Proc. SIGGRAPH) 27(3), Article 67, 2008. Section 3 ("Edge-Preserving Smoothing via WLS"), Equation 6 specifically (not Eq. 3-5 as cited — Eq. 3 is the WLS energy functional, Eq. 4 its quadratic-form matrix rewrite, Eq. 5 the resulting linear system u=(I+λL_g)^{-1}g; the weight formula itself is Eq. 6, one paragraph after Eq. 5). PDF verified at https://szeliski.org/papers/Farbman-SG08.pdf (mirrors also at cs.huji.ac.il/~danix/epd/epd.pdf and cs.jhu.edu/~misha/ReadingSeminar/Papers/Farbman08.pdf).
verified.note:
Core math claim CONFIRMED verbatim against the primary source PDF. Eq. 6 reads exactly: a_x,p(g) = (|∂ℓ/∂x(p)|^α + ε)^{-1}, a_y,p(g) = (|∂ℓ/∂y(p)|^α + ε)^{-1}, where ℓ is the log-luminance channel, "the exponent α (typically between 1.2 and 2.0) determines the sensitivity to the gradients of g, while ε is a small constant (typically 0.0001) that prevents division by zero in areas where g is constant." This matches the claim's alpha in [1.2, 2.0] and eps ~0.0001 exactly.

Two corrections to the citation as given:
1. Equation range is wrong. The weight formula is Eq. 6, not "Eq. 3-5." Eqs. 3-5 are the WLS energy (Eq.3), its matrix/quadratic form (Eq.4), and the linear-system solution u=(I+λL_g)^{-1}g (Eq.5) — all upstream of the weight definition. Section number (3) and section title ("Edge-Preserving Smoothing via WLS") are correct.
2. Provenance: the paper explicitly attributes this exact weighting scheme to prior work — "we define them in the same manner as in [Lischinski et al. 2006]" (D. Lischinski, Z. Farbman, M. Uyttendaele, R. Szeliski, "Interactive Local Adjustment of Tonal Values," SIGGRAPH 2006). So Farbman et al. 2008 is the paper that packages/popularizes this weight construction inside the general multi-scale WLS decomposition framework and is the standard citation for it, but the specific a_x/a_y formula itself originates in Lischinski et al. 2006. Citing Farbman 2008 alone (as the claim does) is the conventional/defensible citation in the local-tone-mapping literature and not wrong, but a fully precise citation would name both papers.

The claim's closing rationale — that WLS's spatially-varying weight lets smoothing degree scale independently per pixel "without trading off spatial vs range kernel size the way the bilateral filter must" — is a faithful paraphrase of the paper's own stated motivation in Section 2 (Background): the paper walks through a synthetic step-edge example showing the BLF "trades off its edge-preservation abilities with its smoothing abilities" via σs/σr, contrasted with WLS achieving "both finer and coarser smoothing... while preserving the step edges, and without introducing obvious artifacts." That framing is Section 2, not Section 3, but it is the paper's own argument, correctly attributed in spirit.

Everything else in the claim (paper title, author list, venue SIGGRAPH 2008, section title, the exact functional form, alpha range, eps value, "log-luminance") checks out against the primary source text extracted directly from the PDF.

checked.claim: Durand & Dorsey (2002) identify the mechanistic root cause of small/thin halos as a signal-to-noise / data-sufficiency problem at edges: pixels straddling a strong edge have very little statistical support in the bilateral filter's local window (the normalization factor k(s) becomes small), so the filtered estimate there is effectively made from too little data -- and they propose fixing exactly this uncertainty by detecting low-k pixels and locally blending toward a blurred correction, rather than by tuning sigma_r/sigma_s.
checked.detail: Exact text: 'edge-preserving contrast reduction can still encounter small halo artifacts for antialiased edges or due to flare around high-contrast edges. We noticed similar problems on some synthetic as well as real images. We propose an explanation in terms of signal/noise ratio. These small halos correspond to pixels where there is not enough information in the neighborhood to decouple the large-scale and the small-scale features. Indeed, the values at the edges span the whole range between the upper and the lower values, and there are very few pixels in the zone of proper data of the influence function. We thus compute a local estimator with very little data, and the variance is quite high. Fortunately, bilateral filtering provides a direct measure of this uncertainty: The normalization factor k in Eq. 6 is the sum of the influence of each pixel. We can therefore use it to detect dubious pixels that need to be fixed. We use the log of this value because it better extracts uncertain pixels. The fixing strategy we use is simple. We compute a low-pass version J~ of the output J of the bilateral filter, using a small Gaussian kernel (2 pixels in practice), and we assign to a pixel the value of a linear interpolation between J and J~ depending on the log of the uncertainty.' Figure 19 shows this halved the haloing artifacts in a vertical highlight/lamp scene.
checked.source: Durand, Dorsey, 'Fast Bilateral Filtering for the Display of High-Dynamic-Range Images' (SIGGRAPH 2002), Section 5.3 'Uncertainty' and Figure 19

verified.corrected_source: Durand, F. and Dorsey, J., "Fast Bilateral Filtering for the Display of High-Dynamic-Range Images," ACM Transactions on Graphics (Proc. SIGGRAPH) 21(3), 2002, pp. 257-266. Section 5.3 "Uncertainty" (p.6), with the visual demonstration in Figure 19 ("Zoom of Fig. 17... without / with uncertainty fix / uncertainty") and a second illustration of the uncertainty map in Figure 18 (hotel room scene, rightmost panel). PDF verified directly at https://people.csail.mit.edu/fredo/PUBLI/Siggraph2002/DurandBilateral.pdf (author's own MIT CSAIL page).
verified.note:
Verified by reading the actual PDF text (not a secondary summary). The claim is substantively CONFIRMED almost verbatim against Section 5.3 "Uncertainty":

Exact primary-source text: "We propose an explanation in terms of signal/noise ratio. These small halos correspond to pixels where there is not enough information in the neighborhood to decouple the large-scale and the small-scale features. Indeed, the values at the edges span the whole range between the upper and the lower values, and there are very few pixels in the zone of proper data of the influence function. We thus compute a statistical estimator with very little data, and the variance is quite high. Fortunately, bilateral filtering provides a direct measure of this uncertainty: The normalization factor k in Eq. 6 is the sum of the influence of each pixel. We can therefore use it to detect dubious pixels that need to be fixed. We use the log of this value because it better extracts uncertain pixels. The fixing strategy we use is then simple. We compute a low-pass version J-tilde of the output J of the bilateral filter, using a small Gaussian kernel (2 pixels in practice), and we assign to a pixel the value of a linear interpolation between J and J-tilde depending on the log of the uncertainty."

This confirms every substantive clause of the claim: (a) signal/noise-ratio framing, (b) k(s) as the statistical-support measure that goes low at edges, (c) the fix is detecting low-k pixels via log(k) and blending toward a small-Gaussian blurred version J-tilde, (d) this is presented as distinct from and not reducible to sigma_r/sigma_s tuning (sigma_r=0.4 and sigma_s constant at 2% of image size are discussed separately in Section 6.1 as fixed global smoothing-scale defaults, never proposed as a halo remedy).

One correction to the citation as given: Section number (5.3) is exactly right. The figure citation "Figure 19" alone is defensible (it is the clearest before/after/uncertainty-map demonstration, captioned "the haloing artifacts... are dramatically reduced") but incomplete — Figure 18 (hotel room) also explicitly shows an uncertainty-map panel ("The rightmost image shows the uncertainty") for a second scene. The fuller and more accurate citation is "Figures 18-19," not "Figure 19" alone. No other inaccuracies found; nothing in the claim contradicts the source, and no misattribution to a different paper/author.

checked.claim: Durand & Dorsey give explicit numeric guidance for the base contrast reduction and range parameter that performed consistently well across their test scenes: base contrast factor of 5 (log-domain compression of the base layer) and sigma_r = 0.4 (with sigma_s held constant at 2% of image size), noting these values were surprisingly consistent across very different scenes.
checked.detail: Exact text: 'In practice, a base contrast of 5 worked well for all our examples... The value sigma_r = 0.4 performed consistently well for all our experiments. Again, this property is quite important because the user does not have to set a complex parameter... As a conclusion, the only user-controlled parameters of our method are the overall brightness and the base contrast.' sigma_s is kept at a fixed 2% of the image diagonal/size: 'This is important since it allows us to keep sigma_s constant to a value of 2% of the image size.'
checked.source: Durand, Dorsey, 'Fast Bilateral Filtering...' (SIGGRAPH 2002), Section 6 'Contrast reduction' and Section 7 'Discussion'

verified.corrected_source: Durand, F. and Dorsey, J., "Fast Bilateral Filtering for the Display of High-Dynamic-Range Images," ACM Transactions on Graphics (Proc. SIGGRAPH) 21, 3 (2002), Section 6 "Contrast reduction" only (page 7 of the SIGGRAPH proceedings PDF, https://people.csail.mit.edu/fredo/PUBLI/Siggraph2002/DurandBilateral.pdf). NOT Section 7.
verified.note:
Fetched and OCR'd the actual paper PDF (MIT/CSAIL host, Fredo Durand's page) and verified against raw text. The three numeric claims are real and quoted almost verbatim from the primary source, but ALL of them live in Section 6 "Contrast reduction," not Section 7 "Discussion" as cited — so the citation is refuted on section attribution even though the numbers themselves are accurate.

Exact primary-source sentences (Section 6, p.7, continuous paragraph run):
1. "In practice, a base contrast of 5 worked well for all our examples, but in some situations where lights sources are visible, one might want to vary this setting."
2. "...the scale σs of the spatial kernel had little influence on the result. This is important since it allows us to keep σs constant to a value of 2% of the image size."
3. "The value σr = 0.4 performed consistently well for all our experiments. Again, this property is quite important because the user does not have to set a complex parameter."

Section 7 "Discussion" (immediately follows, same page) is entirely forward-looking future-work text — uncertainty treatment, a more principled fixing method, more elaborate base-layer compression functions, white balance, perceptual aspects, animated sequences — and contains NO numeric restatement of base contrast=5, σs=2%, or σr=0.4. So citing "Section 7 'Discussion'" for these numbers is wrong; the correct citation is Section 6 alone.

Additional smaller issue: the claim's phrase "surprisingly consistent across scenes" is not the authors' language. The paper says the values "performed consistently well for all our examples/experiments" and frames this as important because it spares the user from tuning "a complex parameter" — it does not use "surprising" or express surprise; that's an added editorial gloss not present in the primary source. The underlying substance (values held up across their diverse test scenes: Grove D/C, Memorial, Hotel room, Vine, Fog, Window, Interior, etc., per Fig. 14's scene list) is accurate.

So: the three numbers (base contrast=5, σs=2% of image size, σr=0.4) are accurate and correctly attributed to Durand & Dorsey SIGGRAPH 2002. The claim is REFUTED only on: (a) wrong section citation (Section 7 Discussion contains none of this; it's all Section 6 Contrast reduction), and (b) an unsupported "surprisingly" characterization not in the authors' own wording.

checked.claim: Aubry/Paris/Hasinoff/Kautz/Durand (2014) formally show that a two-level local Laplacian filter reduces to an UNNORMALIZED variant of the bilateral filter, and attribute the local Laplacian filter's halo-resistance directly to this absence of the 1/W_p normalization factor: the unnormalized filter has a weaker effect exactly where the bilateral filter's normalizer W_p is small (i.e. at edges / ambiguous data, the same locations Durand & Dorsey flag as low-confidence), producing softer but cleaner edges instead of spurious detail-layer content.
checked.detail: Key equations: two-level LLF reduces to O_p = I_p + sum_q Gbar_sigma_p(q-p) f(I_q-I_p)(I_q-I_p) [Eq.10], structurally identical to the bilateral filter Op=Ip+ (1/W_p) sum_q G_ss(q-p) G_sr(Iq-Ip)(Iq-Ip) [Eq.6] MINUS the 1/W_p normalization. They define the 'unnormalized bilateral filter' UBF_p = I_p + sum_q Gbar_ss(q-p)G_sr(Iq-Ip)(Iq-Ip) [Eq.14], and show it equals a spatially-varying blend UBF_p=(1-alpha_p)I_p + alpha_p*BF_p [Eq.15] where alpha_p = sum_q Gbar_ss(q-p)G_sr(Iq-Ip) is close to 1 in uniform regions and drops near edges (exactly where W_p is small) -- 'This also explains why the unnormalized bilateral filter has a weaker effect near discontinuities.' Figure 5 in the paper demonstrates on a soft edge that 'The standard filter over-sharpens the edge (center) and introduces non-negligible content in the detail layer (right). These spurious variations are a source of halos and gradient reversals in applications such as tone mapping. In comparison, the unnormalized filter produces a cleaner edge with almost no spurious content in the detail layer.'
checked.source: Aubry, Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, Durand, 'Fast Local Laplacian Filters: Theory and Applications' (ACM TOG 2014), Section 2.1 'Analysis of local Laplacian filters' and Section 2.2 'Unnormalized bilateral filtering', Eq. 6/10/14/15, Figure 5

verified.corrected_source: Confirmed as cited, no correction needed: Aubry, M., Paris, S., Hasinoff, S. W., Kautz, J., and Durand, F. "Fast Local Laplacian Filters: Theory and Applications." ACM Transactions on Graphics 33(5), Article 167, 2014 (DOI 10.1145/2629645). Section 2.1 "Analysis of local Laplacian filters" (subsection 2.1.1 "Single-scale filter", two-level pyramid derivation, Eq. 10) and Section 2.2 "Unnormalized bilateral filtering" (Eq. 14 defines UBF_p, Eq. 15 gives the spatially-varying-blend form), with the bilateral filter itself rewritten via kernel symmetry in Eq. 6. Halo/edge behavior comparison is in Figure 5 (soft-edge and small-detail synthetic test, standard BF in red vs. unnormalized in green).
verified.note:
Full-text-verified against the actual paper PDF (self-hosted preprint at imagine.enpc.fr/~aubrym/projects/llf/texts/2014-fast-laplacian-filter.pdf, textually identical to the published ACM TOG 33(5) article 167, doi:10.1145/2629645). Every specific claim element checks out verbatim or near-verbatim:

1. Two-level LLF -> bilateral-like form: Section 2.1.1 explicitly frames "a two-level pyramid" and derives Eq. 10, stating "This equation shows that the two-level local Laplacian filter computes a local average in the same spirit as the bilateral filter (Eq. 6)."

2. "Only difference is normalization": verbatim, "the two-level local Laplacian filter becomes almost the same as the bilateral filter—the only difference is that the weights are not normalized by W1p. This simple modification defines a new filter we call the unnormalized bilateral filter which we examine in Section 2.2."

3. Eq. 14 (Section 2.2): UBF_p = I_p + sum_q Gsigma_s(q-p) Gsigma_r(I_q-I_p)(I_q-I_p) — exact equation number confirmed.

4. Eq. 15: UBF_p = (1-alpha_p) I_p + alpha_p BF_p — exact equation number confirmed, described as "spatially varying blend between the input and the result of the bilateral filter," derived "directly from Equations 6 and 14."

5. Weaker effect where W_p is small / at edges: verbatim, "this unnormalized version has a weaker effect when the sum of the weights W is small. This occurs when the center pixel is different from many of its neighbors, which typically happens at edges."

6. Durand & Dorsey (2002) robust-statistics / low-confidence framing: verbatim, "Durand and Dorsey [2002] interpret W from a robust statistics standpoint and explain that when it is small, the bilateral filter returns an estimate based on limited data, which causes the artifacts that appear at some strong edges. The unnormalized version can be interpreted as a filter that is weaker at those ambiguous locations (Fig. 5)."

7. Figure 5 citation: confirmed correct — its caption is precisely the soft-edge and small-isolated-detail synthetic comparison (standard bilateral in red vs. unnormalized in green), concluding "the unnormalized filter produces a cleaner edge with almost no spurious content in the detail layer" and explaining the softer-but-cleaner tradeoff, matching the claim's "softer but cleaner edges instead of spurious detail-layer content" almost word for word.

No discrepancies found. One minor calibration note for future citations: this halo-transition-vs-pyramid-depth behavior across MORE than 2 levels is separately illustrated in Figure 7 (not cited in the claim, and not needed — the claim's Fig. 5 citation is specifically about the 2-level/single-scale soft-edge case, which is correct). The claim as written required no correction.

checked.claim: The original local Laplacian filter (Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz 2011) defines sigma_r as the exact edge/detail threshold via a piecewise remapping function r(i) split at |i-g0|=sigma_r into a detail-modifying branch r_d and an edge-modifying branch r_e, both required to be continuous and monotonically increasing at the boundary (r_d(g0 +/- sigma_r) = r_e(g0 +/- sigma_r)) -- this continuity constraint at the sigma_r boundary is what the paper credits for avoiding 'spurious discontinuities' that would otherwise seed halos.
checked.detail: Exact formulas: r_d(i) = g0 + sign(i-g0)*sigma_r*f_d(|i-g0|/sigma_r) [Eq.1, detail branch, f_d maps [0,1]->[0,1]], r_e(i) = g0 + sign(i-g0)*(f_e(|i-g0|-sigma_r)+sigma_r) [Eq.2, edge branch, f_e defined on [0,infinity)]. For detail manipulation they use f_d(delta)=delta^alpha (alpha<1 enhances, alpha>1 smooths), f_e=identity. For tone mapping they use f_e(a)=beta*a (edge/dynamic-range compression), applied on log intensity. To fight noise amplification when alpha<1, they blend: f_d(delta) = tau*delta^alpha + (1-tau)*delta where tau is a smooth step, 0 below 1% of max intensity and 1 above 2%, explicitly to prevent boosting near-zero-amplitude noise into visible artifacts. Text: 'The advantage of the functional forms defined in Equations 1 and 2 is that they ensure that r is continuous and increasing... to avoid the creation of spurious discontinuities, we constrain rd and re to be continuous by requiring that rd(g0+/-sigma_r) = re(g0+/-sigma_r).'
checked.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, 'Local Laplacian Filters: Edge-aware Image Processing with a Laplacian Pyramid' (SIGGRAPH 2011 / TOG), Section 4 'Local Laplacian Filtering' and Section 5.2 'Detail Manipulation', Eq. 1-2

verified.corrected_source: Paris, S., Hasinoff, S. W., and Kautz, J. "Local Laplacian Filters: Edge-aware Image Processing with a Laplacian Pyramid." ACM Transactions on Graphics 30, 4 (Proc. SIGGRAPH 2011), Article 68. Section 4 ("Local Laplacian Filtering"), subsection "Remapping Function for Gray-scale Images," p.5 (right column) and Eq. 1-2 (p.5-6). PDF: https://people.csail.mit.edu/sparis/publi/2011/siggraph/Paris_11_Local_Laplacian_Filters.pdf
verified.note:
Core math/mechanism CONFIRMED verbatim in Section 4, p.5: "We differentiate their treatment by defining two functions r_d and r_e, such that r(i) = r_d(i) if |i-g0| <= sigma and r(i) = r_e(i) otherwise... Since we require r to be monotonically increasing, r_d and r_e must have this property as well. Furthermore, to avoid the creation of spurious discontinuities, we constrain r_d and r_e to be continuous by requiring that r_d(g0 +/- sigma) = r_e(g0 +/- sigma)." Eq. 1 (r_d) and Eq. 2 (r_e) immediately follow. So: the piecewise split at |i-g0|=sigma_r, the continuity condition r_d(g0+/-sigma_r)=r_e(g0+/-sigma_r), the monotonicity requirement, and the phrase "spurious discontinuities" are all real and exact -- not fabricated.

Two corrections needed before the claim can be marked confirmed as stated: (1) Citation is mis-scoped. All of this -- the r_d/r_e definition, the continuity/monotonicity constraints, and Eq. 1-2 -- lives entirely in Section 4 ("Local Laplacian Filtering"). Section 5.2 ("Detail Manipulation," confirmed as its real title) comes later and only specializes f_d (setting f_d(Delta)=Delta^alpha for the S-curve), it does not restate or re-derive the continuity constraint. Citing "Section 4 ... and Section 5.2 ... Eq. 1-2" incorrectly implies the constraint spans both sections. It should cite Section 4 only for the constraint/Eq.1-2, with 5.2 optionally cited separately for the detail S-curve. (2) The paper never explicitly says this continuity constraint is "what avoids halos" -- that specific causal sentence does not exist in the text. The paper says the constraint avoids "spurious discontinuities" (Sec 4), and separately claims the overall method is halo-free (Abstract; Sec 5.4 Discussion: "our approach produces consistent results without halos, whereas the other methods either create halos or fail to exaggerate detail," re: Fig. 15 comparison to Li 2005 and Farbman 2008). The paper never sentence-level equates "spurious discontinuities at the sigma_r boundary" with "halos" -- linking the two is a plausible inference but is not textually attributable to the paper. Verdict: refuted as a precise citation (wrong section span, and an unsupported causal attribution to "halos" specifically), though the underlying math/mechanism description is accurate and real.

checked.claim: In Hessel & Morel's cross-filter ranking, the Fast Local Laplacian filter (FLL) is the ONLY one of seven tested filters (guided filter, fast bilateral filter, domain transform, WLS, FLL, TV-L1, L0 gradient minimization) to pass all four artifact tests, while the guided filter fails contrast halo AND luminance halo, and WLS specifically fails compartmentalization -- giving a concrete, citable prior that the LLF family is structurally the most halo/pooling-resistant of the compared operators, though not perfectly immune, and specifying the exact fair-comparison parameters used.
checked.detail: Exact text: 'We find in the fused scores in Figure 11 that the fast local Laplacian filter (FLL) is the best and the domain transform (DT), image smoothing with L0 gradient minimization (IS-L0) and the guided filter (GF) are the worst of the tested methods... the staircase effect invalidated IS-L0 and FBF; the compartmentalization invalidated DT, TV-L1, WLS; the contrast halo invalidated GF; the luminance halo invalidated DT, GF, and IS-L0. Only LLF succeeded passing the five artifact tests.' Their fair-comparison methodology cross-calibrates all filters to the same detail-layer PSNR=16.23dB via PSNR(u0-u1) = -10*log10((1/sqrt(N))*||u0-u1||^2) (Eq.16), and lists FLL's calibrated parameters: number of samples for range subsampling S=64, l_max=6 pyramid levels, sigma_r=0.103 (vs. e.g. guided filter r=40, eps=0.075^2; WLS alpha=1.2, lambda=0.5).
checked.source: Hessel, Morel, 'Quantitative Evaluation of Base and Detail Decomposition Filters...' (arXiv:1808.09411, 2018), Section V (Table I) and Section VI-C 'Summary and conclusion'

verified.corrected_source: Hessel, C. and Morel, J.-M., "Quantitative Evaluation of Base and Detail Decomposition Filters Based on their Artifacts," arXiv:1808.09411v1 [cs.CV], 28 Aug 2018. Table I appears in Section V ("Setting the Parameters of Each Filter"). The pass/fail enumeration and Table II ("Unacceptable artifacts (in red) in the tested filters") and the sentence "Only LLF succeeded passing the [four] artifact tests" appear in Section VI-B ("Final score and ranking"), immediately followed by Section VI-C ("Summary and conclusion on the comparative experiments") which restates the same result in prose. So Table I -> Sec V is correctly cited; the "Only [F]LL[F] passed all tests" line and Table II are in VI-B, not VI-C (VI-C only gives the higher-level restatement) — a minor section-attribution imprecision in the claim, not a substantive error.
verified.note:
All four substantive sub-claims verified true against the primary-source PDF (text extraction + rendered page images of pp.9-11).

1) FLL is uniquely the only filter passing all four artifact tests: CONFIRMED. Table II (p.11) shows FLL's column entirely green (no objectionable artifacts) while all six other filters (DT, IS-L0, FBF, GF, TV-L1, WLS) have at least one red cell. Verbatim text (p.10): "Only LLF succeeded passing the five artifact tests." (NB: the paper itself has a typo here -- "five" should read "four"; every other mention in the paper, including the abstract, intro, and Section VII conclusion, says "four artifacts"/"four canonical artifacts." The claim under test correctly says "four artifact tests," which is more accurate than the paper's own typo, not less.) Also note the paper's own abbreviation inconsistency: it defines "FLL" in Table I ("Fast local Laplacian filter") but this one summary sentence typos it as "LLF" -- the claim's use of "FLL" matches the paper's formal/consistent abbreviation.

2) Guided filter (GF) fails BOTH contrast halo and luminance halo: CONFIRMED. Table II shows GF red in both rows. Text: "The guided filter is the only filter among the tested ones that have a contrast halo artifact" (Sec IV) and "the luminance halo invalidated DT, GF, and IS-L0" (Sec VI-B).

3) WLS specifically fails compartmentalization (and only that): CONFIRMED. Table II shows WLS red only for compartmentalization. Text: "the compartmentalization invalidated DT, TV-L1, WLS" and WLS's compartmentalization score is used to set the acceptability threshold for that artifact ("WLS defines the score threshold for the compartmentalization").

4) Exact fair-comparison parameters: CONFIRMED via Table I (p.9): GF r=40, eps=.075^2; FBF sigma_s=40, sigma_r=.1178 (S=64 intensity samples); DT sigma_s=40, sigma_r=0.1166 (recursive filter, 3 iterations); WLS alpha=1.2, lambda=0.5; FLL lmax=6, sigma_r=0.103 (S=64 range samples); TV-L1 lambda=.205; IS-L0 kappa=2, lambda=.002. Cross-calibration methodology (Sec V): parameters set semi-automatically -- default author values + coherent spatial-scale settings, then the detail-strength parameter tuned per filter so the detail layer's L2-norm/PSNR is equalized across filters, targeting PSNR = 16.23 dB on five calibration images (Fig. 9).

One imprecision in the claim's citation: the pass/fail enumeration and Table II itself sit in Section VI-B ("Final score and ranking"), not VI-C. VI-C ("Summary and conclusion on the comparative experiments") immediately follows and restates the result at a higher level ("the fast local Laplacian filter (FLL) is the best and [DT], [IS-L0], and [GF] are the worst"), so citing VI-C is defensible as "where the conclusion is stated" but the primary Table II / detailed pass-fail text is technically VI-B. This is a minor section-attribution slip, not a factual error about the paper's findings.

Files used for verification (scratchpad, session-local): hessel_morel.pdf (fresh download from arxiv.org/pdf/1808.09411), hessel_morel.txt / hessel_morel_fresh.txt (pdftotext -layout extraction, byte-identical), page10-10.png and page11-11.png (250dpi page renders confirming Table I, Table II, and the "Only LLF succeeded..." sentence verbatim and in color).

checked.claim: TMQI (Yeganeh & Wang, 2013) combines a multi-scale structural fidelity term S and a statistical naturalness term N via Q = a*S^alpha + (1-a)*N^beta with fitted constants a=0.8012, alpha=0.3046, beta=0.7088, but its structural fidelity component is a modified SSIM built around SIGNAL-STRENGTH comparison via a psychophysically-derived nonlinear sigma->sigma' mapping (not an edge-overshoot or gradient-sign term), meaning TMQI's S component is NOT purpose-built to detect halos -- it captures loss/gain of structural detail visibility but would not specifically flag a smooth overshoot band unless that overshoot altered local standard deviation enough to cross the visibility-threshold nonlinearity.
checked.detail: Local structural fidelity: S_local(x,y) = [2*sigma'_x*sigma'_y+C1)/(sigma_x'^2+sigma_y'^2+C1)] * [(sigma_xy+C2)/(sigma_x*sigma_y+C2)] (Eq.1), where sigma_x,sigma_y are local std devs in the HDR/LDR patches and sigma_xy is their cross-correlation; C1=0.01, C2=10. The KEY departure from vanilla SSIM is that sigma_x, sigma_y are first passed through a nonlinear mapping sigma' = (1/sqrt(2*pi)*theta_sigma) * integral_{-inf}^{sigma} exp[-(x-tau_sigma)^2/(2*theta_sigma^2)] dx (Eq.8), derived from a psychometric detection-probability model (Galton's ogive, Crozier's law k=tau_s/theta_s~3, contrast sensitivity function A(f)=2.6*[0.0192+0.114f]*exp[-(0.114f)^1.1] evaluated at CSF frequencies {16,8,4,2,1} cycles/degree for 5 pyramid scales), so that 'significant' contrast (above visibility threshold) maps toward sigma'=1 and 'insignificant' contrast maps toward sigma'=0. Overall S is a weighted geometric mean across scales: S = product_{l=1}^{L} S_l^{beta_l} (Eq.10) with L=5 and {beta_l}={0.0448,0.2856,0.3001,0.2363,0.1333}, mirroring Wang et al.'s MS-SSIM weights. Naturalness N = (1/K)*P_m*P_d (Eq.13) is built from Gaussian-fitted brightness statistics (mu_m=115.94, sigma_m=27.99) and Beta-fitted contrast statistics (alpha_d=4.4, beta_d=10.1) over ~3000 natural images -- entirely global/statistical, has no spatial locality and thus cannot localize a halo band at all.
checked.source: Yeganeh, Wang, 'Objective Quality Assessment of Tone-Mapped Images' (IEEE TIP vol.22 no.2, 2013), Sections II-A 'Structural Fidelity' (Eq.1-10) and II-B 'Statistical Naturalness' (Eq.11-14)

verified.corrected_source: Confirmed as cited — no correction needed. Primary source: H. Yeganeh and Z. Wang, "Objective Quality Assessment of Tone-Mapped Images," IEEE Transactions on Image Processing, vol. 22, no. 2, pp. 657-667, February 2013 (DOI: 10.1109/TIP.2012.2221725). PDF verified directly at https://ece.uwaterloo.ca/~z70wang/research/tmqi/TIP_TMQI.pdf. The claim's pointer to "Sections II-A 'Structural Fidelity' (Eq.1-10) and II-B 'Statistical Naturalness' (Eq.11-14)" is correct — those are the actual section titles and equation ranges in the published paper (Eq. 1-10 = structural fidelity incl. the sigma->sigma' nonlinear mapping machinery of Eqs. 2-8 and multiscale pooling Eqs. 9-10; Eq. 11-13 = naturalness Gaussian/Beta fits; Eq. 14 = the combined Q formula, which is technically just past the II-B boundary in Section II-C but is the formula referenced).
verified.note:
Every specific, checkable element of the claim was verified against the primary-source PDF text (pages 657-662):

1. FORMULA AND CONSTANTS — exact match. Eq. 14: "Q = aS^α + (1-a)N^β". Paper's closing line of Sec. II-C: "we select a = 0.8012, α = 0.3046, and β = 0.7088 as our final model parameters." (Note: the paper prints β = 0.7088, not 0.7089 or similar — claim's 0.7088 is exact.)

2. STRUCTURAL FIDELITY MECHANISM — claim is correct and well-supported. Sec. II-A states verbatim: "The first term in (1) compares signal strength and is modified from that of the SSIM definition... To distinguish between significant and insignificant signal strength, we pass the local standard deviation σ through a nonlinear mapping, which results in the σ' value employed in (1)... the nonlinear mapping is related to the visual sensitivity of contrast, which has been extensively studied... Psychometric functions describing the detection probability of signal strength have been employed to model the data taken from psychophysical experiments." This directly substantiates "signal-strength comparison via a psychophysically-derived nonlinear sigma->sigma' mapping" — the derivation chain (Eqs. 2-8) runs through Galton's ogive/cumulative normal distribution (Eq. 2), Crozier's law (Eq. 3, 7), and a contrast sensitivity function (Eq. 4, citing Kelly's CSF measurement) to build the σ->σ' mapping (Eq. 8), which is then plugged into the modified-SSIM local fidelity measure (Eq. 1).

3. NOT AN EDGE-OVERSHOOT/GRADIENT-SIGN TERM — confirmed by absence. Nowhere in Sections II-A/II-B (or the rest of the paper through Section III, which I also read) does the text mention gradients, edges, overshoot, ringing, or "halo" as a mechanism. S is built entirely from local σ (patch standard deviation) and cross-correlation (σ_xy) terms borrowed from SSIM's contrast/structure comparisons — a signal-strength/variance-based measure, not a directional-derivative or sign-based edge detector.

4. HALO NOT DISCUSSED — confirmed. No occurrence of "halo" found in the extracted primary text (abstract, intro, full Sec. II, start of Sec. III). This supports the claim's inferential conclusion that TMQI's S is not purpose-built to flag halos and would only respond to a halo if the overshoot's local σ crossed the CDF-based visibility-threshold nonlinearity in Eq. 8 — this is a reasonable, unforced reading of the mechanism, not an overreach.

One minor imprecision in the claim's citation, not the substance: it says the combining formula and section II-B "cover" Eq. 11-14, but Eq. 14 (the Q combination formula) actually appears in Section II-C ("Quality Assessment Model"), one subsection after II-B ends at Eq. 13. This is a trivial section-boundary slip, not a misrepresentation of content — II-B genuinely covers Eq. 11-13 (naturalness), and Eq. 14 is presented in the immediately-following short subsection. I did not treat this as grounds to reject the claim since the technical content is 100% accurate and this is the kind of section-boundary rounding a careful reader would consider acceptable shorthand. Flagged here for transparency.

Cross-verified independently via the dvolgyes/TMQI open-source Python reimplementation (github.com/dvolgyes/TMQI, src/TMQI.py), whose code matches the paper exactly: `Q = a*(S**Alpha) + (1-a)*(N**Beta)` with a=0.8012, Alpha=0.3046, Beta=0.7088, and a `_Slocal` function that computes sigma1p/sigma2p via `norm.cdf` (the Eq. 8 sigma->sigma' nonlinearity) fed through a CSF formula matching Eq. 4 of the paper — confirming the implementation faithfully encodes the signal-strength/psychophysical-nonlinearity mechanism described in the text, not an edge/gradient mechanism.

checked.claim: TMQI was validated against human subjective TMO rankings and achieves only moderate rank correlation (mean KRCC~0.70, mean SRCC~0.81 in cross-validation; KRCC=0.66-0.72, SRCC=0.78-0.82 on independent test databases), and its own leave-one-out cross-validation shows TMQI's per-image-set correlation is only 'within +/-1 standard deviation from the mean over all subjects' -- i.e. it behaves like one representative human rater, not a precision instrument, which is a caveat against treating raw Q as a fine-grained halo detector in an automated test-suite gate.
checked.detail: Table I (6-dataset leave-one-out CV): mean KRCC=0.7333 (std 0.1632), mean SRCC=0.8333 (std 0.1211). Table II (independent 14-TMO dataset): Structural Fidelity alone KRCC=0.6923/SRCC=0.7912; Naturalness alone KRCC=0.3846/SRCC=0.5385 (naturalness alone is a poor predictor); combined Overall Quality KRCC=0.7179/SRCC=0.8187. Third validation (15 image sets x 8 TMOs, 20 subjects): average TMQI performance KRCC=0.6649, SRCC=0.7963, compared to average-single-subject performance KRCC=0.7014 (std 0.1606), SRCC=0.8143 (std 0.1368) -- TMQI sits inside, not above, the human inter-rater spread.
checked.source: Yeganeh, Wang, 'Objective Quality Assessment of Tone-Mapped Images' (IEEE TIP 2013), Section III 'Validation of Quality Assessment Method', Tables I-III

verified.corrected_source: Yeganeh, H. & Wang, Z., "Objective Quality Assessment of Tone-Mapped Images," IEEE Transactions on Image Processing, Vol. 22, No. 2, Feb. 2013, pp. 657-667, DOI 10.1109/TIP.2012.2221725. Section III "Validation of Quality Assessment Method," specifically Table I (p.662, cross-validation using data from [34]), Table II (p.662, performance evaluation using data from [10],[37]), and Table III (p.663, "Performance Evaluations Using 15 Image Sets and 8 TMOs" — the third experiment). PDF: https://ece.uwaterloo.ca/~z70wang/research/tmqi/TIP_TMQI.pdf
verified.note:
Verified directly against the primary-source PDF (11 pages, read in full for Sections II-III including Tables I-III). The claim's core textual quote is genuine but its numbers and attributions are garbled/conflated across three distinct experiments in the paper:

1. "mean KRCC~0.70, mean SRCC~0.81 in cross-validation" is WRONG. Table I is the actual leave-one-out cross-validation (used only to fit TMQI's parameters a, alpha, beta on 6 data sets from ref [34]) and reports mean KRCC=0.7333, mean SRCC=0.8333 (std 0.1632/0.1211) -- not 0.70/0.81. The numbers ~0.70/~0.81 actually match Table III's "Mean Subject Performance" row (KRCC 0.7014, SRCC 0.8143), which is the average human-rater self-consistency baseline in a completely different (third) experiment on a 15-set/8-TMO database the authors built themselves -- not a cross-validation result at all, and not TMQI's own score (TMQI's own average in Table III is KRCC 0.6649, SRCC 0.7963).

2. "KRCC=0.66-0.72, SRCC=0.78-0.82 on independent test databases" does not appear in the paper as a stated range. Table II (the actual independent-database test, using data from refs [10],[37]) reports single point values, not a range: Overall Quality KRCC=0.7179, SRCC=0.8187 (with Structural Fidelity and Statistical Naturalness sub-scores much lower/wider: KRCC 0.3846-0.6923, SRCC 0.5385-0.7912 -- these are different quality components, not "independent test databases" plural). No table in the paper presents a 0.66-0.72 / 0.78-0.82 range as such.

3. The '+/-1 standard deviation from the mean over all subjects' quote IS verbatim and accurate (p.662: "the KRCC and SRCC values of TMQI are well within the range of +/-1 standard deviation from the KRCC and SRCC values of the mean over all subjects. This indicates that TMQI behaves quite similarly to an average subject.") -- but it describes Table III (the third, self-collected 15-set/20-subject/8-TMO experiment), NOT the leave-one-out cross-validation (Table I / Section II-C), which is a separate procedure used only for parameter fitting and has no per-subject-spread analysis. The claim's framing "its own leave-one-out cross-validation shows... within +/-1 SD" misattributes this finding to the wrong experiment.

Net effect on the argument: the claim's rhetorical point (TMQI validated only to moderate rank correlation, behaves like one representative rater rather than a precision instrument, caution against using raw Q as a fine-grained automated halo-detector gate) is directionally defensible and the underlying facts point the same way, but as cited -- "Section III, Tables I-III" with those specific number pairs and that specific attribution of the +/-1 SD finding to "leave-one-out cross-validation" -- the citation is inaccurate. Corrected version: TMQI's actual leave-one-out cross-validation (Table I) gives mean KRCC=0.7333, SRCC=0.8333 (std 0.1632/0.1211); the independent-database test (Table II) gives Overall Quality KRCC=0.7179, SRCC=0.8187; and the separate third experiment (Table III, 15 sets/8 TMOs) shows TMQI's own KRCC/SRCC (avg 0.6649/0.7963) falling within +/-1 SD of the mean per-subject KRCC/SRCC (avg 0.7014 +/-0.1606 / 0.8143 +/-0.1368) -- the last of which is the genuine basis for the "behaves like one representative human rater" characterization, not the cross-validation procedure.

checked.claim: The mechanism by which LLF avoids halos is architectural, not a filter-shape trick: for every output Laplacian pyramid coefficient (x0,y0,l0), the algorithm re-renders an entire intermediate full-resolution image by applying the point-wise remap r_{g0,sigma_r} to the ORIGINAL image (not the already-processed one), builds a fresh Laplacian pyramid from that intermediate image, and copies only the single coefficient at (x0,y0,l0) into the output pyramid -- repeated independently per coefficient (O(N log N) via subregion restriction). This means each coefficient's neighborhood context is always the true original signal, so there is no compounding/propagation of a previous coefficient's error into a neighbor -- this is what the paper credits for halo-freedom versus 'a naive approach [that] is to scale down the signal uniformly... equivalent to scaling all pyramid coefficients by the same factor'.
checked.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz 2011, Section 4 ('Local Laplacian Filtering') and Algorithm 1 (pseudocode); Figure 5 ('Overview of the basic idea').
checked.detail: This is the load-bearing mechanism the user should compare their own implementation against: their described variant ('ABSOLUTE calibrated tone curve added to the Gaussian pyramid RESIDUAL at ~64px scale') sounds architecturally closer to a two-scale (base+detail) decomposition than to LLF's full per-coefficient re-rendering -- which the project's own v10c doc already flags as losing the halo-free property once the pointwise nonlinearity is decoupled from a separately-extracted base (see finding on v10c below).

verified.corrected_source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, "Local Laplacian Filters: Edge-aware Image Processing with a Laplacian Pyramid," ACM SIGGRAPH 2011 (ACM TOG 30(4)). PDF verified at https://people.csail.mit.edu/sparis/publi/2011/siggraph/Paris_11_Local_Laplacian_Filters.pdf. Algorithm 1 and Figure 5 ("Overview of the basic idea of our approach") are correctly cited and appear in Section 4 ("Local Laplacian Filtering," p.5) exactly as described. HOWEVER the quoted sentence "a naive approach... is to scale down the signal uniformly... equivalent to scaling all the pyramid coefficients by the same factor" is in Section 3.2 ("Pyramid-based Range Compression," p.4), NOT Section 4 -- cite it as Sec. 3.2, with Sec. 4 as the generalized method it motivates.
verified.note:
Mechanical/algorithmic claim is CONFIRMED verbatim against the primary source: Algorithm 1 explicitly builds R0 (the sub-region needed for the target coefficient) from the ORIGINAL input image I on every iteration, remaps it, builds a fresh sub-pyramid, and copies only the single coefficient at (x0,y0,l0) -- exactly as described. Figure 5's title and caption match exactly. O(N log N) via K x K sub-region restriction is confirmed in the text immediately following Algorithm 1.

Two corrections needed:
(1) SECTION MISATTRIBUTION: the "naive approach... scale down the signal uniformly... equivalent to scaling all pyramid coefficients by the same factor" quote is verbatim-correct but lives in Section 3.2, not Section 4. Section 4 is the generalized method; the naive-approach strawman appears earlier in the 1D motivating analysis (Sec 3.2), which Sec 4 explicitly builds on ("our more general approach... (Sec. 4)").

(2) OVERREACH ON CAUSAL FRAMING: the paper never states, in these or equivalent words, that "each coefficient's neighborhood context is always the true original signal, so there is no compounding/propagation of a previous coefficient's error into a neighbor" as the explicit stated reason for halo-freedom vs. the naive approach. That specific causal-mechanism language is the claim-writer's paraphrase/inference, not a quote. The paper's closest supporting statement is in Sec 3.2 ("Incorporating Local Processing," p.4): "because this processing is applied to the whole original signal, our method effectively takes into account global correlations between coefficients of the Laplacian pyramid as well" -- this supports the spirit of the claim but doesn't use "compounding" or "error propagation" framing. Also, the paper's explicit critique of the "scale down uniformly" naive approach is that it produces DULL results (attenuated details), not halos; the halo failure mode is attributed to a DIFFERENT naive approach discussed on the same page -- Li et al. 2005's coefficient-thresholding scheme ("thin but unsightly rim halos"). The claim conflates these two distinct strawmen into one. Net: algorithmic description and figure/algorithm citations are accurate and directly verifiable; the causal explanation and one section number are the claimant's synthesis/error layered on top, not the paper's own words. Overall confirmed=true because the core testable mechanism claim (re-render from original per-coefficient, discard sub-pyramid, no propagation) is exactly what Algorithm 1 does -- but corrected_source should flag Sec 3.2 for the naive-approach quote and note the halo-causal-explanation is inferential, not a paper quote.

checked.claim: The Aubry et al. 2014 'Fast Local Laplacian Filters' paper proves that a two-level local Laplacian filter with f=Gaussian(sigma_r) is nearly identical to bilateral filtering EXCEPT it is unnormalized (no division by the sum-of-weights term W), and that this lack of normalization is specifically what suppresses halos/gradient reversal versus the standard (normalized) bilateral filter: 'these spurious variations are a source of halos and gradient reversals in applications such as tone mapping. In comparison, the unnormalized filter produces a cleaner edge with almost no spurious content in the detail layer.'
checked.source: Aubry, Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, Durand, 'Fast Local Laplacian Filters: Theory and Applications', ACM TOG 33(5), 2014, Section 2.1.1-2.1.3 and Figure 5 caption; also proposes a new 'unnormalized bilateral filter' (Eq. 14-15) derived from this insight.
checked.detail: Concrete mechanism: standard bilateral filter output BF_p = (1/W_p) * sum_q G_spatial(q-p) * G_range(I_q-I_p) * I_q; the unnormalized variant UBF_p = I_p + sum_q G_spatial(q-p) * G_range(I_q-I_p) * (I_q-I_p) drops the 1/W_p division. At edges/corners/isolated pixels W_p is small (few similar neighbors), so normalization causes the normalized filter to 'return an estimate based on limited data' (citing Durand & Dorsey 2002's robust-statistics framing), which is exactly the mechanism the paper blames for bilateral-filter halos and reverse gradients on thin features (e.g. window frames). This gives a directly transferable mitigation: if the user's residual-injection scheme involves any spatially-weighted local averaging, check whether that averaging is normalized -- de-normalizing (blending toward the unweighted original when local support is thin) is a documented halo-reduction mechanism.

verified.corrected_source: Aubry, M., Paris, S., Hasinoff, S. W., Kautz, J., and Durand, F. "Fast Local Laplacian Filters: Theory and Applications." ACM Transactions on Graphics 33(5), Article 167, 2014. Primary-source PDF verified: https://imagine.enpc.fr/~aubrym/projects/llf/texts/2014-fast-laplacian-filter.pdf (author's copy, page 5). Exact locations: the "nearly identical to bilateral filtering except unnormalized" derivation is in Section 2.1.1 "Single-scale filter" (culminating in the sentence beginning "choose f = Gσr, then the two-level local Laplacian filter becomes almost the same as the bilateral filter—the only difference is that the weights are not normalized by 1/Wp"), with the Recap in 2.1.3 and a forward-pointer to "Section 2.2." The formal "unnormalized bilateral filter" proposal (UBF, with the alternative-interpretation blend form) is in Section 2.2 "Unnormalized bilateral filtering," and is defined by Eq. 14 (UBF_p = I_p + Σ_q Ḡσs(q−p) Gσr(Iq−Ip)(Iq−Ip)) and Eq. 15 (UBF_p = (1−αp) I_p + αp BF_p) — exactly as claimed, on page 5-6. The exact quote is the caption of Figure 5 on page 5: "...These spurious variations are a source of halos and gradient reversals in applications such as tone mapping. In comparison, the unnormalized filter produces a cleaner edge with almost no spurious content in the detail layer."
verified.note:
All substantive claims are CONFIRMED verbatim against the primary-source PDF (which is the authors' copy of the ACM TOG 33(5) 2014 paper, matching page/section/equation numbering). The direct quote is exact and correctly located as the Figure 5 caption on page 5. Equations 14-15 are exactly as cited and do define the new "unnormalized bilateral filter." The lack-of-normalization-suppresses-halos causal claim is explicitly the paper's own framing (§2.2 discussion: "the unnormalized version can be interpreted as a filter that is weaker at those ambiguous locations [i.e. edges, where W is small]... greatly reduces the artifacts as shown in Figure 7").

One minor discrepancy to flag: the citation's "Section 2.1.1-2.1.3" range is correct for the *derivation and discussion* of the near-equivalence (2.1.1 Single-scale filter, 2.1.2 Multi-scale filter, 2.1.3 Recap all exist under 2.1 "Analysis of local Laplacian filters" exactly as such), BUT the *formal proposal* of the new "unnormalized bilateral filter" with Eq. 14-15 is NOT inside 2.1.x — it is in Section 2.2 "Unnormalized bilateral filtering," a top-level sibling section, not a subsection of 2.1. A precise citation should read "Section 2.1.1-2.1.3 (analysis) and Section 2.2 (formal UBF proposal, Eq. 14-15), plus the Figure 5 caption (p.5)" rather than folding everything into "2.1.1-2.1.3." This is a small citation-scope imprecision, not a misattribution — every quoted/paraphrased claim checks out against the true text, section headers, and equation numbers.

checked.claim: Aubry et al. 2014 report, as an explicit measured finding (not theory), that 'halos may be observed at intermediate [pyramid] depths whereas they are typically not an issue at small and large scales' when transitioning from the 1-level unnormalized bilateral filter to the full multi-level local Laplacian filter -- i.e. halo severity is NON-MONOTONIC in the number of pyramid levels processed, worst at some intermediate depth, consistent with Trentacoste et al. 2012.
checked.source: Aubry et al. 2014, Section 2.2 ('Unnormalized bilateral filtering'), final paragraph, citing consistency with Trentacoste, M. et al. 2012 ('Unsharp Masking, Countershading and Halos: Enhancements or Artifacts?').
checked.detail: This is a specific, load-bearing calibration finding for the user's own '~64px scale' choice: pyramid-depth is not a monotonic knob for halo severity in this filter family -- there is a documented intermediate-depth worst case. The user should measure their own overshoot metric across a sweep of injection scales (not assume that a single fixed 64px scale is safely away from the bad zone) the same way the repo's own v10c doc measured guided-filter radius r=8 vs r=16.

verified.corrected_source: Aubry, M., Paris, S., Hasinoff, S. W., Kautz, J., and Durand, F. 2014. "Fast Local Laplacian Filters: Theory and Applications." ACM Transactions on Graphics 33(5), Article 167. Section 2.2 ("Unnormalized bilateral filtering"), Discussion paragraph, p.6 (right column) -- the sentence immediately follows the reference to Figure 7 (pyramid-depth sweep from 1-level unnormalized bilateral filter to full multi-level local Laplacian filter). The identical finding is independently restated verbatim in the Figure 7 caption on p.7. Full text (Discussion paragraph): "Figure 7 shows the effects of varying the pyramid depth to transition from the unnormalized bilateral filter with one level to local Laplacian filtering with a full pyramid. Halos may be observed at intermediate depths whereas they are typically not an issue at small and large scales. This observation is consistent with the findings of Trentacoste et al. [2012]." Figure 7 caption: "Unsightly halos are visible at intermediate depths (c); this observation is consistent with the results of Trentacoste et al. [2012]." Bibliography entry confirms citation target: Trentacoste, M., Mantiuk, R., Heidrich, W., and Dufrot, F. 2012. "Unsharp Masking, Countershading and Halos: Enhancements or Artifacts?" Proceedings of Eurographics (also Computer Graphics Forum 31(2), pp.555-564). Source PDF verified at https://imagine.enpc.fr/~aubrym/projects/llf/texts/2014-fast-laplacian-filter.pdf (downloaded and text-extracted directly, page ~6-7).
verified.note: Claim confirmed essentially verbatim against the primary source. I downloaded the actual paper PDF (WebFetch failed on it -- 16.7MB exceeds the 10MB fetch limit -- so I curl'd it directly and ran pdftotext) and located the exact sentence. The wording "Halos may be observed at intermediate depths whereas they are typically not an issue at small and large scales" is a near-exact quote (I checked character-for-character against the extracted text -- it matches). It IS presented as an empirical/observed finding tied to a specific figure (Fig. 7, a 1/2/4/8-level pyramid-depth sweep), not abstract theory, and it IS explicitly stated to be "consistent with the findings of Trentacoste et al. [2012]" -- both in the body text and, independently, in the Figure 7 caption, which strengthens rather than weakens the claim. The bibliography entry matches the cited Trentacoste et al. 2012 "Unsharp Masking, Countershading and Halos: Enhancements or Artifacts?" (Eurographics/CGF 31(2)) exactly as described in the claim. Only a trivial correction to the "citation locus" description: the sentence isn't isolated as literally the very last sentence of Section 2.2 divorced from context -- it's the sentence directly following the Figure 7 reference within that section's closing "Discussion" paragraph, and it is echoed verbatim in the Figure 7 caption itself (which sits a page later due to figure placement in the two-column layout). This is a non-substantive nuance; the section attribution (2.2, final/Discussion paragraph) is correct. No refutation found on any point of substance -- non-monotonicity (worst at intermediate depth, not worst at 1-level or full-depth) is exactly what the source states.

checked.claim: HDR+ deliberately did NOT use local Laplacian filters (Aubry et al. 2014) for its production local tone mapping despite being co-authored by the same lab (Hasinoff is an author on both), explicitly citing computational cost as the reason, choosing a variant of Mertens/Kautz/Van Reeth 2007 exposure fusion instead: 'employing fixed point arithmetic wherever possible. These same concerns preclude using algorithms with global iteration..., large or dynamic spatial support..., or expensive tone mapping (e.g., local Laplacian filters [Aubry et al. 2014]).'
checked.source: Hasinoff et al. 2016, Section 7 ('Results') / 'Performance' paragraph.
checked.detail: This is a direct production engineering tradeoff data point: a full LLF-quality local tone operator was considered and rejected for a shipping mobile pipeline purely on cost/latency grounds (not quality), with exposure fusion chosen as the cheaper alternative that still needed halo mitigation (see next findings).

verified.corrected_source: Hasinoff, S.W., Sharlet, D., Geiss, R., Adams, A., Barron, J.T., Kainz, F., Chen, J., Levoy, M. "Burst photography for high dynamic range and low-light imaging on mobile cameras." ACM Transactions on Graphics 35(6), Article 192 (Proc. SIGGRAPH Asia 2016). DOI: 10.1145/2980179.2980254. Section 7 "Results," run-in subheading "Performance" (not a numbered subsection — ACM TOG papers of this era use bold run-in paragraph headers, e.g. "Performance", "Failure cases"). Verified directly against the author-hosted PDF at https://people.csail.mit.edu/hasinoff/pubs/HasinoffEtAl16-hdrplus.pdf, which matches the published ACM DOI.
verified.note: Claim CONFIRMED against primary source, word-for-word. Full exact sentence from the paper (Section 7, "Performance" paragraph): "To make our pipelines fast enough to deploy on mobile devices, we have selected algorithms for their computational efficiency. This means avoiding non-local communication and data dependencies that preclude parallelization, consuming as little memory as possible, and employing fixed point arithmetic wherever possible. These same concerns preclude using algorithms with global iteration (e.g., FlexISP [Heide et al. 2014]), large or dynamic spatial support (e.g., BM3D [Dabov et al. 2007b]), or expensive tone mapping (e.g., local Laplacian filters [Aubry et al. 2014])." The claim's quoted fragment (joined by an ellipsis) preserves the source's exact wording and order — not a splice from elsewhere. The Mertens/exposure-fusion choice is independently confirmed in Section 6 "Finishing" ("The tone mapping method we have chosen is a variant of exposure fusion [Mertens et al. 2007]") and restated in the Introduction ("We use a variant of exposure fusion [Mertens et al. 2007], because it is computationally efficient and produces natural-looking images; however, other algorithms are possible."). Authorship overlap is real: Hasinoff co-authored both "Fast Local Laplacian Filters: Theory and Applications" (Aubry, Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, Durand — ACM TOG 2014) and this HDR+ paper; the Aubry paper is cited in HDR+'s own reference list (AUBRY, M., PARIS, S., HASINOFF, S. W., KAUTZ, J., AND DURAND, F. 2014. Fast local laplacian filters: Theory and applications. TOG.). One small imprecision in the claim as phrased: the paper doesn't frame this as literally "choosing Mertens INSTEAD OF Laplacian filters" in one single contrastive sentence — the exposure-fusion choice is stated in Section 6, and the "why not Laplacian filters" cost-efficiency rationale is stated separately in Section 7's Performance paragraph. Both statements are genuine and consistent, but they are two distinct passages in the paper rather than one single juxtaposed sentence; a reader should not assume the paper explicitly writes "we chose Mertens instead of Aubry" as one contiguous clause — that linkage is a reasonable synthesis by the claimant, not a verbatim single-sentence pairing in the source.

checked.claim: The original exposure-fusion paper (Mertens, Kautz, Van Reeth 2007) gives the exact per-pixel weight formula the user needs for a fusion-weight-normalization mitigation: W_ijk = (C_ijk)^wC * (S_ijk)^wS * (E_ijk)^wE, product (not sum) of three measures -- Contrast C (absolute value of a Laplacian filter response on grayscale), Saturation S (per-pixel standard deviation across R,G,B), and Well-exposedness E (per-channel Gaussian centered at 0.5 with sigma=0.2, multiplied across channels: exp(-(i-0.5)^2 / (2*0.2^2))) -- then normalizes weights to sum to 1 across the N images at each pixel before blending.
checked.source: Mertens, Kautz, Van Reeth, 'Exposure Fusion', Pacific Graphics 2007, Section 3.1 ('Quality Measures') and Section 3.2 ('Fusion'), Eq. numbered as W_ij,k formula and normalization sum-to-one equation.
checked.detail: The multiplicative (AND-like) combination is explicitly chosen over a linear combination (OR-like) so that a region must score well on ALL three measures simultaneously to get high fusion weight -- directly useful as a mitigation pattern: gating a local adjustment's strength on multiple independent local-content measures (not just one, e.g. not just 'is this pixel dark') reduces the chance an isolated small bright/dark region alone drives a large local weight and pools adjustment around it.

verified.corrected_source: Mertens, T., Kautz, J., & Van Reeth, F. (2007). "Exposure Fusion." In Proceedings of the 15th Pacific Conference on Computer Graphics and Applications (Pacific Graphics 2007), pp. 382–390. Section 3 "Exposure Fusion", Section 3.1 "Quality Measures" (defines C, S, E — all unnumbered inline/display text, no equation numbers), Section 3.2 "Fusion" (contains the normalization equation, also unnumbered). The ONLY numbered equation in this part of the paper is Eq. (1), which is the final weighted-blending equation R_ij = Σ_{k=1}^{N} Ŵ_ij,k · I_ij,k — not the weight-power-function formula and not the normalization formula.
verified.note: Verified directly against the primary source PDF (Stanford-hosted copy of the paper, cross-checked against the paper's known page-3/page-4 layout). The core technical content of the claim is CONFIRMED accurate: (1) the weight formula is exactly W_ij,k = (C_ij,k)^ωC × (S_ij,k)^ωS × (E_ij,k)^ωE, a product (not sum) of Contrast, Saturation, Well-exposedness, appearing in Section 3.1 "Quality Measures" under the heading "For each pixel, we combine the information from the different measures into a scalar weight map using multiplication... a power function"; (2) Contrast C = absolute value of a Laplacian filter response on the grayscale image; (3) Saturation S = per-pixel standard deviation across R,G,B channels; (4) Well-exposedness E = per-channel Gaussian centered at 0.5, sigma=0.2, exp(-(i-0.5)^2/(2*sigma^2)), multiplied across channels — matches claim's formula verbatim (paper writes 2σ² in the denominator, same as claim's 2*0.2^2); (5) normalization to sum-to-one across N images at each pixel is given in Section 3.2 "Fusion" as Ŵ_ij,k = [Σ_{k'=1}^{N} W_ij,k']^{-1} · W_ij,k, immediately before the blending step. HOWEVER, one specific sourcing detail in the claim is WRONG and must be corrected: the claim asserts these two key equations (the weight-product formula and the normalization formula) are "numbered" equations in the paper. In the actual paper only ONE equation carries a number in this vicinity — Eq. (1) — and Eq. (1) is the final blending/reconstruction equation R_ij = Σ Ŵ_ij,k·I_ij,k, not the weight formula and not the normalization formula. Both the weight-power-function equation and the Ŵ normalization equation are unnumbered display equations. So the claim's substance (formulas, section titles 3.1/3.2, sigma=0.2, product not sum, multiply-across-channels for E) is fully correct and directly citable to the primary source, but the "Eq. numbered as..." sourcing description is inaccurate/overclaimed and should be dropped or corrected to note these are unnumbered equations, with Eq. (1) being the separate final blending equation.

checked.claim: Mertens et al. 2007 explicitly document the exact halo-generation mechanism from naive Gaussian-smoothed weight-map blending, and give the fix: naive per-pixel weighted averaging (Eq 1, R_ij = sum_k W_hat_ijk * I_ijk) produces 'disturbing seams' wherever weights vary quickly; smoothing the weight map with a Gaussian filter removes seams but 'results in undesirable halos around edges, and spills information across object boundaries'; the adopted fix is Burt-Adelson-style multiresolution (Laplacian pyramid) blending, where blending is performed independently PER PYRAMID LEVEL using the Gaussian pyramid of the (unsmoothed) weight map as per-level blend weights: L{R}^l_ij = sum_k G{W_hat}^l_ijk * L{I}^l_ijk.
checked.source: Mertens, Kautz, Van Reeth 2007, Section 3.2 ('Fusion') and Section 3.3 ('Discussion'); Figure 4 caption explicitly compares (a) input, (b) naive weighted blend with seams, (c) Gaussian-blurred-weight blend with halos, contrasted with the paper's pyramid blend.
checked.detail: Key mechanistic insight stated directly: 'Since the blending equation is computed at each scale separately, sharp transitions in the weight map can only affect sharp transitions [that] appear in the original images (e.g. edges). Conversely, flat regions in the original images will always have negligible coefficient magnitude, and are thus not affected by possibly sharp variations in the weight function.' This is the precise multi-scale weighting mechanism the user asked about -- doing the weight-modulated blend at every pyramid scale (rather than blurring the weight map once at full-res) automatically confines the effect of a sharp weight-map boundary to locations that already have a real image edge there, preventing the blur from leaking a flat region's adjustment into an adjacent flat region (the 'pooling' failure mode).

verified.corrected_source: Mertens, T., Kautz, J., & Van Reeth, F. (2007). "Exposure Fusion." Proceedings of the 15th Pacific Conference on Computer Graphics and Applications (Pacific Graphics 2007), pp. 382–390. All of the cited technical content — Eq. 1 (R_ij = Σ_k Ŵ_ijk I_ijk), the "disturbing seams" / Gaussian-blur-causes-halos discussion, and the multiresolution (Laplacian-pyramid-of-images / Gaussian-pyramid-of-weights) fusion equation L{R}^l_ij = Σ_k G{Ŵ}^l_ijk L{I}^l_ijk — appears in **Section 3.2 "Fusion" only** (and its Figure 4). Section 3.3 is titled "Discussion" but covers comparison to *other* seamless-blending/image-fusion literature (gradient-domain blending, Burt & Kolczynski's compositing method, Goshtasby's block-based technique) — it does not contain the halo-mechanism explanation or Figure 4.
verified.note: Fetched and read the primary source PDF directly (web.stanford.edu/class/cs231m/project-1/exposure-fusion.pdf, the authors' paper). Every direct quote and equation in the claim is verbatim-accurate: Eq.1's naive weighted-average formula, the exact phrase "disturbing seams will appear... Wherever weights vary quickly," the exact phrase "results in undesirable halos around edges, and spills information across object boundaries," the Figure 4 caption content (naive=seams, Gaussian-blur=halos, contrasted with multiresolution), and the per-pyramid-level blend equation using the Gaussian pyramid of the (unsmoothed) weight map as blend weights against the Laplacian pyramid of the images. The ONE factual error in the claim is the section attribution: it says the halo-generation mechanism and fix are documented in "Section 3.2 ('Fusion') and Section 3.3 ('Discussion')" — but Section 3.3 has nothing to do with this; the entire mechanism (naive blend -> seams -> Gaussian-smoothed weights -> halos -> pyramid fix), including Figure 4 and both key equations, lives entirely within Section 3.2. Section 3.3 "Discussion" instead compares the multiresolution approach to unrelated prior seamless-blending techniques (gradient-domain fusion, Burt & Kolczynski, Goshtasby) and notes a separate low-frequency brightness artifact (Fig. 6). Because the claim explicitly asserts dual-section sourcing that is verifiably false, confirmed=false despite every quoted string and equation being accurate.

checked.claim: Joint Bilateral Upsampling (Kopf, Cohen, Lischinski, Uyttendaele, SIGGRAPH 2007) is the standard mitigation for edge-aware upsampling from a coarse-resolution processed/weight map back to full resolution, and is documented as specifically avoiding the 'blocking and halo artifacts of other upsampling methods' (e.g. naive bilinear/bicubic upsampling of a low-res solution) by filtering the upsample using a high-resolution guidance image's edges rather than the low-res solution's own (blurred) edges.
checked.source: Kopf, Cohen, Lischinski, Uyttendaele, 'Joint Bilateral Upsampling', ACM TOG 26(3) / SIGGRAPH 2007.
checked.detail: Directly applicable if the user's pyramid residual injection at ~64px scale is later upsampled/broadcast back to full resolution using a simple pyramid-collapse (Gaussian upsample): substituting a joint/cross-bilateral upsample guided by the full-resolution luminance in place of the plain Gaussian-kernel upsample step is the standard literature fix for halos introduced specifically at the upsampling stage (as opposed to halos from the coarse-scale processing itself).

verified.corrected_source: Kopf, J., Cohen, M. F., Lischinski, D., and Uyttendaele, M. "Joint Bilateral Upsampling." ACM Transactions on Graphics 26(3), Article 96 (Proc. SIGGRAPH 2007). Verified via full-text PDF (Univ. of Konstanz KOPS repository copy, matches ACM DL record doi:10.1145/1276377.1276497). The verbatim "blocking and halo artifacts" sentence is the caption of Figure 2 (the Tone Mapping results figure), not the abstract: "Note that JBU does not exhibit the blocking and halo artifacts of the other upsampling methods" — where "the other upsampling methods" compared in that figure are Nearest Neighbor, Gaussian Interpolation, and Bicubic Interpolation applied to a low-resolution exposure map. The abstract itself states the general motivation (smoothness-prior upsampling "typically suffer[s] from blurring of sharp edges") and lists "adaptive tone mapping" among the four demonstrated applications (stereo depth, colorization, tone mapping, graph-cut composition).
verified.note: Claim is confirmed both in substance and via an exact verbatim quote traced to primary source. One correction: the "blocking and halo artifacts of other upsampling methods" wording lives in the Figure 2 caption (the tone-mapping figure specifically, comparing JBU vs. Nearest Neighbor/Gaussian/Bicubic upsampling of a low-res exposure map), not in the abstract as a generic framing statement — citing it as "documented in the paper" is accurate, but citing it as "the abstract says" would be wrong. The core mechanistic claim (JBU filters using the high-res guidance image's edges via the range kernel g(||Ĩ_p−Ĩ_q||), rather than the low-res solution's own edges) is directly confirmed by Equation 3 and the surrounding text in Section 3, which is presented as a straightforward extension of the joint/cross-bilateral filter (Eq. 2) where the range filter operates on a second guidance image instead of the signal being filtered. No refutation found; all checked assertions hold against the primary source text.

checked.claim: Gradient-domain HDR compression (Fattal, Lischinski, Werman, SIGGRAPH 2002) is presented in the literature as 'almost free of halo and gradient reversal artifacts' by construction, because it manipulates and re-integrates the image GRADIENT FIELD directly (attenuating large gradient magnitudes via a multiscale attenuation function Phi derived from a Gaussian pyramid of log-luminance, then solving a Poisson equation to reconstruct pixel values) rather than manipulating pixel intensities or pyramid coefficients and reconstructing by simple pyramid collapse.
checked.source: Fattal, Lischinski, Werman, 'Gradient Domain High Dynamic Range Compression', ACM TOG 21(3) / SIGGRAPH 2002; described secondarily via search-engine synthesis of the paper (PDF at cs.huji.ac.il/~danix/hdr/hdrc.pdf was retrieved but corrupted/unparseable in this session, so formula details are secondary-sourced).
checked.detail: The Fattal02 implementation's beta parameter (recommended range 0.8-0.96, default ~0.9, per the pfstmo_fattal02 command-line tool documentation) directly controls the gradient-attenuation strength/detail-vs-halo tradeoff: 'values below 1.0 strengthen local contrast and enhance details' and 'values below 0.5 lead to very strong amplification of small contrast' (i.e. noise amplification), with the tool's own docs noting overly strong local-contrast settings 'might produce slightly dark image corners' -- a first-class documented halo-adjacent side effect of over-tuning the detail-preservation parameter, structurally analogous to the user's H3 'edge haloing on highlight recovery'. Because I could not get clean text extraction from the primary PDF this session, treat the exact functional form of Phi as needing confirmation against the original paper before citing it as a formula in code comments.

verified.corrected_source: Fattal, R., Lischinski, D., and Werman, M. "Gradient Domain High Dynamic Range Compression." ACM Transactions on Graphics 21(3), pp. 249–256, SIGGRAPH 2002 (Hebrew University of Jerusalem). verified.note: CONFIRMED with one correction. So downstream text should attribute the strong "no halo artifacts arise" framing to the paper's Section 4 mechanism-level argument, and should not use "almost free of" as a direct quote — replace with either the true verbatim abstract phrasing or an explicitly marked paraphrase. checked.claim: The exposure gain applied per pixel is NOT a single lookup — it's a weighted sum over 9 EV octave controls (-8..0 EV, 1 EV apart), where each octave's contribution is weighted by a Gaussian of the radial distance in log2 space between the pixel's (blurred) exposure and that octave's center; this Gaussian-series interpolation is what gives smooth, continuous, infinite-order blending across octave boundaries instead of hard-edged EV buckets.

The radius of the kernel is user-defined and can be tweaked to get a smoother interpolation (possibly generating oscillations), or a more monotonous one." Confirmed in code: CHANNELS=9, centers_params = {-8,-7,-6,-5,-4,-3,-2,-1,0} EV; `smoothing` field (default M_SQRT2_F ≈ 1.414) is the Gaussian kernel sigma used in `build_interpolation_matrix()`.
