checked.claim: darktable's 'exposure-independent guided filter' (EIGF, default variant) was created specifically because the original He/Sun/Tang guided filter blurs shadows and highlights by different effective amounts (blur strength is exposure-dependent), which is itself a source of asymmetric halo/selectivity behavior between dark and bright regions; EIGF normalizes by dividing local variance by the squared center-pixel value (regularization = variance / center_pixel_value^2) to equalize blur strength across the tonal range.
checked.source: GitHub darktable-org/darktable issue #9808 "diffuse: exposure independent edge awareness" and darktable manual masking-tab description: "guided filter: The guided filter is exposure-sensitive, meaning that shadows tend to be blurred more than highlights." / "eigf (default): The exposure-independent guided filter solves the problem of the original guided filter."

verified.corrected_source: Primary source for the EIGF mechanism/motivation: darktable source code doc comment in src/common/eigf.h (introduced in commit c167f9d8f25180fd7a583528505a0764ba71bc9d, authored by contributor "rawfiner", committed by Aurélien Pierre, Oct 2020; shipped as default in darktable 3.4, Dec 2020), NOT GitHub issue #9808. Manual wording corroborated at https://docs.darktable.org/usermanual/development/en/module-reference/processing-modules/tone-equalizer/ (module-reference/processing-modules/tone-equalizer). Issue #9808 (https://github.com/darktable-org/darktable/issues/9808, "diffuse: exposure independent edge awareness", opened Aug 2021) is a real issue with that exact title, but it proposes porting the already-existing tone-equalizer EIGF normalization (regularization = variance / center_pixel_value^2) into the unrelated "diffuse or sharpen" module — and Aurélien Pierre explicitly rejected it there ("it didn't work great because it oversharpened the shadows... I think the current behaviour is more physically-plausible"), after which the submitter closed it unmerged. Current src/iop/diffuse.c contains no such normalization.
verified.note:
The technical content is accurate and well-supported: eigf.h's original doc comment reads "As variance depends on the exposure, the original guided filter preserves much better the edges in the highlights than in the shadows... instead of using variance directly to determine 'a', we use a ratio: variance / (pixel_value)^2 ... We kept variance / (pixel_value)^2." The darktable manual independently states "the guided filter is exposure-sensitive, meaning that shadows tend to be blurred more than highlights" (guided filter) vs. "The exposure-independent guided filter solves the problem of the original guided filter, in that it makes the degree of blurring independent of the exposure" (eigf, default). So the physics/motivation/formula parts of the claim are all correct and well-sourced.

BUT the citation is wrong in a materially misleading way: EIGF was created and shipped as the tone-equalizer default in Oct-Dec 2020, roughly 10 months before issue #9808 was even opened (Aug 2021). #9808 cannot be "the reason EIGF was created" — it postdates EIGF's existence. #9808 is instead a request to reuse the already-existing EIGF idea in a second, different module (diffuse-or-sharpen), and that specific proposal was tested and rejected by the lead author, then closed without being merged. Citing #9808 as the origin story for EIGF is a misattribution — it's citing a rejected spinoff proposal as if it were the founding issue. Verdict: refuted as stated (wrong/misleading citation), though the underlying technical claim about EIGF itself is true and better sourced to src/common/eigf.h and the darktable 3.4 release / user manual.

checked.claim: The core He/Sun/Tang guided filter (as implemented in darktable's src/common/guided_filter.c, used as the toneequal fallback) computes per-pixel linear coefficients a,b via local mean/variance/covariance of the guide image over a box window of radius w, with the regularization parameter eps = sqrt_eps^2 added to the variance diagonal (Sigma_0_0 = var_rr - guide_r^2 + eps) before solving a 3x3 system by Cramer's rule; eps controls smoothing strength (larger eps -> more averaging / less edge adherence, mechanically identical to the feathering tradeoff) and w controls the spatial scale of averaging.
checked.source: src/common/guided_filter.c: `const float eps = sqrt_eps * sqrt_eps; // this is the regularization parameter of the original papers` and `const float Sigma_0_0 = varpx[VAR_RR] - (guide_r * guide_r) + eps;` with box-mean window radius `w` passed through `_guided_filter_tiling(...)`.

verified.corrected_source: src/common/guided_filter.c (darktable-org/darktable, master branch) does contain the quoted code verbatim: `const float eps = sqrt_eps * sqrt_eps; // this is the regularization parameter of the original papers` (lines ~261, ~474) and `const float Sigma_0_0 = varpx[VAR_RR] - (guide_r * guide_r) + eps;` (line ~174), followed by a Cramer's-rule solve (det0 etc.) inside `_guided_filter_tiling`, with `w` (window size) threaded from the public `guided_filter()` entry point (src/common/guided_filter.h:55). HOWEVER this file/function is called only from src/develop/blend.c (`_develop_blend_process_feather`, for drawn-mask feathering) and src/iop/hazeremoval.c (haze removal) — never from src/iop/toneequal.c. The tone equalizer includes src/common/fast_guided_filter.h instead, a structurally different single-channel implementation (`variance_analyse`: box-mean of {guide, mask, guide*guide, guide*mask}, scalar `a = cov/(var+feathering)`, no 3x3 matrix, no Sigma_0_0, no Cramer's rule) invoked via `fast_surface_blur`/`fast_eigf_surface_blur`. Confirmed via direct read of the cloned repo (github.com/darktable-org/darktable) and full grep of toneequal.c's git history (it has never included common/guided_filter.h). Also, "fallback" in guided_filter.c refers only to `_guided_filter_cl_fallback`, an OpenCL-buffer-copy-to-CPU-host path used when the direct OpenCL kernel implementation fails — unconnected to toneequal.
verified.note: The quoted code snippets and the eps/w mechanical semantics (paper-accurate: larger eps -> more smoothing/less edge fidelity; w -> spatial scale of averaging) are verified byte-accurate and technically sound. But the claim's load-bearing attribution — "used as the toneequal fallback" — is false. This exact He/Sun/Tang guided_filter.c implementation backs drawn-mask feathering (develop/blend.c) and haze removal (iop/hazeremoval.c), not the tone equalizer. Toneequal.c uses a separate, simpler implementation in common/fast_guided_filter.h (single-channel guide, scalar closed-form blend, no Sigma matrix/Cramer's rule at all) via DT_TONEEQ_GUIDED/DT_TONEEQ_EIGF. Separately, the only "fallback" literally named in guided_filter.c is an OpenCL-to-CPU device fallback (_guided_filter_cl_fallback), unrelated to toneequal. Anyone citing this claim to reason about toneequal's halo behavior would be reasoning about the wrong code path.

Their default sigma_r = log(2.5) 'gave consistently good results' and is dynamic-range-independent because it operates in the log domain as a fixed intensity RATIO threshold, not an absolute value.

From a practical standpoint, we advise keeping sigma_r fixed and varying the slope beta between 0... and 1... Unless otherwise specified, we use sigma_r = log(2.5), which gave consistently good results... It does not depend on the dynamic range of the scene.'

SIGGRAPH 2011), Article 68. Section 5.3 "Tone Manipulation" (with the fe=0 clipping definition given earlier in Section 3, "Analysis"). PDF verified at https://people.csail.mit.edu/sparis/publi/2011/siggraph/Paris_11_Local_Laplacian_Filters.pdf. Verbatim: "In our framework, we manipulate large-scale variations by defining a point-wise function modifying the edge amplitude, fe(a) = βa, where β ≥ 0 is a user-defined parameter (Fig. 6)... For tone mapping, we set our filter with α ≤ 1 so that details are preserved or enhanced, and β < 1 so that edges are compressed... From a practical standpoint, we advise keeping σr fixed and varying the slope β between 0, where the local contrast is responsible for most of the dynamic range, and 1, where the global contrast dominates. Unless otherwise specified, we use σr = log(2.5), which gave consistently good results in our experiments. Since we work in the log domain, this value corresponds to a ratio between pixel intensities. It does not depend on the dynamic range of the scene, and assumes only that the input HDR image measures radiance up to scale." The β=0 clipping meaning is defined earlier (Section 3): "clipping edges corresponds to fe = 0, which limits the amplitude of all edges to σr." Figure 6's parameter diagram labels β=1 as the identity/no-modification case for the edge family (row: 0≤β<1 / β=1 / β>1), consistent with β=1 meaning no compression.
verified.note: Claim CONFIRMED, essentially word-for-word accurate, and correctly attributed. I downloaded the actual paper PDF (52MB, from the MIT/Adobe author's own page) and extracted the raw text with pdftotext, then located Section 5.3 and the surrounding Section 3 "Analysis" subsection. Every quoted/paraphrased element checks out verbatim or near-verbatim: (1) fe(a)=βa with β≥0 as the general edge-amplitude function, defined in Fig. 6/Sec 5.3; (2) β=0 clipping-to-sigma_r is stated explicitly in Sec 3 ("clipping edges corresponds to fe = 0, which limits the amplitude of all edges to σr"), not literally in 5.3 but it is the same fe/β machinery the paper cross-references via Fig. 6, so attributing it to 'Section 5.3 Tone Manipulation' is a very minor imprecision (the β=0 clipping semantics are established in Sec 3, then invoked/used in 5.3) — not a misattribution of substance, just of which subsection first defines it; (3) the 'advise keeping σr fixed, vary β between 0 and 1' sentence is exact verbatim; (4) σr=log(2.5) 'gave consistently good results' is exact verbatim; (5) the dynamic-range-independence explanation ('does not depend on the dynamic range of the scene... ratio between pixel intensities') is exact verbatim. checked.claim: Paris et al. reduce cost by capping the intermediate sub-pyramid depth at 5 levels total (applying the remap to a downsampled image starting at level max(0, l0-3) rather than the full-resolution image for coarse output levels) with negligible quality loss — PSNR 30-40 dB vs the uncapped version, imperceptible visually — which directly supports capping/quantizing discretization levels as a valid speed/quality tradeoff, not just a naive approximation.

The resulting images are visually indistinguishable from the full-pyramid process with a PSNR on the order of 30 to 40 dB.'

verified.corrected_source: Paris, S., Hasinoff, S. W., and Kautz, J. "Local Laplacian Filters: Edge-aware Image Processing with a Laplacian Pyramid." ACM Transactions on Graphics 30(4), Article 68 (SIGGRAPH 2011), Section 5.1 "Implementation." Verbatim text (confirmed by extracting text directly from the official PDF at https://people.csail.mit.edu/sparis/publi/2011/siggraph/Paris_11_Local_Laplacian_Filters.pdf): "We use the pyramids defined by Burt and Adelson [1983], based on 5 x 5 kernels. On a 2.26 GHz Intel Xeon CPU, we process a one-megapixel image in about a minute using a single thread. This can be halved by limiting the depth of the intermediate pyramid to at most 5 levels, by applying the remapping to level max(0, l0 - 3) rather than always starting at the full-resolution image. This amounts to applying the remapping to a downsampled version of the image when processing coarse pyramid levels. The resulting images are visually indistinguishable from the full-pyramid process with a PSNR on the order of 30 to 40 dB. checked.claim: darktable applies the exposure map as an ABSOLUTE per-octave EV gain (not a purely relative/multiplicative-only correction relative to neighbors) and its own documentation identifies the mitigation for haloing under this absolute-shift regime as the combination of (a) guided-filter edge-aware blurring of the exposure map itself so the correction is piecewise-constant within contiguous regions rather than pixel-sharp, plus (b) the mask exposure/contrast compensation sliders that re-center and re-scale the map's histogram onto the -8..0 EV control range so the absolute correction magnitude at any given octave stays moderate — i.e. halo suppression for an absolute map is achieved by pre-smoothing the GUIDE (what determines how much correction is applied where) rather than by making the correction itself relative.

checked.claim: Aubry 2014's remapping function r(i,g,sigma_r) is a two-branch piecewise function whose EDGE branch has fixed slope beta (not a clamp), and beta<1 is what compresses dynamic range without abrupt discontinuity; sigma_r is the exact detail/edge threshold.
checked.detail: Equation 1 (as printed): r̃(i) = g + sign(i-g)·σr·(|i-g|/σr)^α  if |i-g| ≤ σr [detail arm]; r̃(i) = g + sign(i-g)·β·(|i-g|-σr) + σr  if |i-g| > σr [edge arm]. Here g is the local Gaussian-pyramid reference value, alpha in [0,∞) controls detail boost (0≤α<1 enhances, α>1 attenuates), beta in [0,∞) controls dynamic-range compression (0≤β<1) or expansion (β>1), sigma_r is the detail/edge threshold. Because beta is a SLOPE applied continuously beyond sigma_r (not a hard clamp to a constant), the edge arm remains monotonically increasing in i, which is required for r to stay a valid (non-crossing) remapping and avoids the sharp coefficient truncation that Li et al. 2005 identified as producing 'thin but unsightly rim halos' (Paris 2011, Sec 3.2, Fig 3 caption).
checked.source: Aubry et al. 2014, Section 1.2, Equation 1 ("Design of the remapping function"); cross-referenced with Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz 2011 SIGGRAPH, Section 4, Equations 1-2

verified.corrected_source: Aubry, Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz & Durand, "Fast Local Laplacian Filters: Theory and Applications," ACM TOG 33(5), 2014, Section 1.2 ("Background on local Laplacian filters"), subsection "Design of the remapping function," Equation 1 (p.4) — verified verbatim against the primary-source PDF at people.csail.mit.edu/sparis/publi/2014/tog/Aubry_14-Fast_Local_Laplacian_Filters.pdf. Cross-reference should be corrected to Paris, Hasinoff & Kautz, "Local Laplacian Filters: Edge-aware Image Processing with a Laplacian Pyramid," SIGGRAPH/ACM TOG 30(4), 2011 — the ABSTRACT two-branch definition (rd/re split at sigma_r) is Section 4, Equations 1-2 (verified against people.csail.mit.edu/sparis/publi/2011/siggraph/Paris_11_Local_Laplacian_Filters.pdf), but the concrete fixed-slope-beta edge function fe(a)=beta*a and the "beta<1 compresses / beta>1 expands dynamic range" statement are in Section 5.3 ("Tone Manipulation"), not Section 4 — Section 4's fe is left as an abstract smooth function.
verified.note:
Adjudication, confirmed with caveat on the cross-reference's section number.

CONFIRMED against exact primary-source text (Aubry et al. 2014, p.4, Eq. 1, verified via direct PDF fetch of people.csail.mit.edu/sparis/publi/2014/tog/Aubry_14-Fast_Local_Laplacian_Filters.pdf):

r̃(i) = { g + sign(i−g) σr(|i−g|/σr)^α   if |i−g| ≤ σr   [detail branch]
        { g + sign(i−g) β(|i−g|−σr) + σr if |i−g| > σr   [edge branch]   ... (1)

Exact quoted prose: "α controls the amount of detail increase (0 ≤ α < 1) or decrease (α > 1), β controls the dynamic range compression (0 ≤ β < 1) or expansion (β > 1), and σr defines the intensity threshold that separates details from edges."

Per-clause verdict:
(1) TWO-BRANCH PIECEWISE — TRUE. Confirmed exactly as claimed, split at |i-g|=σr, continuous at the boundary (both branches equal g+sign(i-g)σr there).
(2) EDGE BRANCH HAS FIXED SLOPE β, NOT A CLAMP — TRUE. The edge branch is g + sign(i−g)·β·(|i−g|−σr) + σr, which is affine/linear in |i−g| with constant slope β. This is NOT a hard clamp (slope 0 / saturation) — a true clamp would correspond to β=0 as one special case, but the general mechanism is a fixed nonzero slope. Not a clamp is correct.
(3) σr IS THE DETAIL/EDGE THRESHOLD — TRUE, stated near-verbatim in both papers ("the intensity threshold that separates details from edges" / "intensity variations smaller than σr should be considered fine-scale details and larger variations are edges").
(4) β<1 COMPRESSES DYNAMIC RANGE WITHOUT ABRUPT DISCONTINUITY — TRUE. Aubry 2014 states β controls "dynamic range compression (0 ≤ β < 1)." Paris 2011 Section 5.3 states explicitly: "β < 1 so that edges are compressed" for tone mapping, and continuity at the branch boundary (rd(g0±σr) = re(g0±σr), stated explicitly in Paris 2011 Sec 4: "to avoid the creation of spurious discontinuities, we constrain rd and re to be continuous") is what avoids the abrupt jump — confirmed.

ONE CORRECTION to the citation: the claim's cross-reference "Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz 2011, Section 4, Equations 1-2" is only partially right. Section 4 Eqs. 1-2 in Paris et al. 2011 are the ABSTRACT/general two-branch split: rd(i)=g0+sign(i−g0)σr·fd(|i−g0|/σr) and re(i)=g0+sign(i−g0)[fe(|i−g0|−σr)+σr], where fd and fe are left as arbitrary smooth functions — β does not appear in Section 4 at all. The concrete choice fe(a)=β·a (which is what produces a literal fixed-slope-β edge branch and licenses the "β<1 compresses dynamic range" claim) is introduced later in Section 5.3 ("Tone Manipulation"): "we manipulate large-scale variations by defining a point-wise function modifying the edge amplitude, fe(a) = βa, where β ≥ 0 is a user-defined parameter... β < 1 so that edges are compressed." So the mathematical content of the claim is accurate, but the more precise citation for the β-instantiated form / dynamic-range-compression statement in the 2011 paper is Section 5.3, not Section 4. Aubry et al. 2014's own Eq. 1 (their Section 1.2) already bakes in this β-linear instantiation as the canonical restatement of "Paris and colleagues'" remapping function, which is presumably why the claim conflates the two — Aubry 2014 Eq 1 is a direct, faithful restatement of the Paris 2011 Sec 5.3 tone-mapping instantiation, not of the abstract Sec 4 form.

- https://people.csail.mit.edu/sparis/publi/2011/siggraph/Paris_11_Local_Laplacian_Filters.pdf (Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz 2011)

checked.claim: Paris/Hasinoff/Kautz 2011 require C1/C0 continuity at the detail/edge boundary sigma_r: rd(g0±σr) = re(g0±σr), which is the formal condition that prevents the remapping function itself from introducing a spurious discontinuity (a synthetic edge) at the sigma_r threshold.
checked.detail: Exact text: 'to avoid the creation of spurious discontinuities, we constrain rd and re to be continuous by requiring that rd(g0 ± σr) = re(g0 ± σr).' The detail-arm formula is rd(i) = g0 + sign(i-g0)·σr·fd(|i-g0|/σr) (Eq. 1) and the edge-arm formula is re(i) = g0 + sign(i-g0)·(fe(|i-g0|-σr) + σr) (Eq. 2), with fd,fe smooth monotone functions satisfying fd(1)=1 to guarantee this junction continuity automatically.
checked.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz, 'Local Laplacian Filters: Edge-aware Image Processing with a Laplacian Pyramid', SIGGRAPH Asia 2011, Section 4, Equations 1-2 and surrounding text

verified.corrected_source: Paris, S., Hasinoff, S. W., and Kautz, J. "Local Laplacian Filters: Edge-aware Image Processing with a Laplacian Pyramid." ACM Transactions on Graphics 30(4), Article 68 (Proc. SIGGRAPH 2011) — note: journal-track SIGGRAPH 2011, not "SIGGRAPH Asia 2011" as cited. Section 4 ("Local Laplacian Filtering"), text immediately preceding Equations 1-2. Exact quote: "We differentiate their treatment by defining two functions rd and re, such that r(i) = rd(i) if |i − g0| ≤ σr and r(i) = re(i) otherwise. Since we require r to be monotonically increasing, rd and re must have this property as well. Furthermore, to avoid the creation of spurious discontinuities, we constrain rd and re to be continuous by requiring that rd(g0 ± σr) = re(g0 ± σr)." (Eq. 1: rd(i) = g0 + sign(i−g0) σr fd(|i−g0|/σr); Eq. 2: re(i) = g0 + sign(i−g0)[fe(|i−g0|−σr) + σr]).
verified.note:
Location/purpose/equation-numbering in the claim are all verified correct against the primary PDF (fetched and text-extracted directly: people.csail.mit.edu/sparis/publi/2011/siggraph/Paris_11_Local_Laplacian_Filters.pdf). The paper is in Section 4 and the boundary-matching condition rd(g0±σr)=re(g0±σr) is stated verbatim, explicitly motivated as avoiding "spurious discontinuities" — i.e. exactly what the claim describes as preventing a synthetic edge at sigma_r.

REFUTED DETAIL: the claim says the paper requires "C1/C0 continuity." The paper requires ONLY C0 (value) continuity — rd(g0±σr) = re(g0±σr), i.e. the two piecewise branches must agree in VALUE at the boundary. There is no equation, sentence, or footnote anywhere in the paper requiring or even discussing derivative/slope matching (C1 continuity) between rd and re at sigma_r. The word "smooth" appears only once, describing fd itself ("fd is a smooth function mapping from [0,1] to [0,1]"), not as a cross-branch C1 constraint. So "C1/C0" should be corrected to just "C0" — the claim overstates the formal continuity order the paper actually imposes.

Secondary correction: the venue in the citation is wrong. This paper (Paris/Hasinoff/Kautz, ACM TOG 30(4) 2011, article 68) was published in the SIGGRAPH 2011 journal track (ACM TOG), not "SIGGRAPH Asia 2011." (There is a separate, later Aubry/Paris/Hasinoff/Kautz/Durand "Fast Local Laplacian Filters" paper (TOG 2014) which is sometimes confused with this one — I confirmed by text-extracting a locally cached PDF that this Aubry paper is NOT the source of the quoted passage; the σr continuity condition is from the original 2011 Paris/Hasinoff/Kautz paper as cited.)

Section/equation numbering (Section 4, Eqs. 1-2) is correct and verified verbatim against the primary source text.

checked.claim: Paris 2011's concrete numeric parameter recommendation for tone mapping: sigma_r = log(2.5) (in the natural-log intensity domain) gave 'consistently good results'; practical guidance is to hold sigma_r fixed and sweep beta in [0,1] to trade off local vs global contrast, with a noise-amplification safeguard that zeroes detail-boost for |delta| below 1% of max intensity, ramping to full effect by 2%.
checked.detail: Exact quotes: '...we advise keeping σr fixed and varying the slope β between 0, where the local contrast is responsible for most of the dynamic range, and 1, where the global contrast dominates. Unless otherwise specified, we use σr = log(2.5), which gave consistently good results in our experiments... this value corresponds to a ratio between pixel intensities [and] does not depend on the dynamic range of the scene.' Noise safeguard (Sec 5.2, 'Reducing Noise Amplification'): 'when α<1, we compute fd(Δ) = τΔ^α + (1-τ)Δ where τ is a smooth step function equal to 0 if Δ is less than 1% of the maximum intensity, 1 if it is more than 2%, with a smooth transition in between.' Additional: for detail manipulation alone they set fe(a)=a (identity, edges untouched); for tone mapping fe(a)=β·a with α≤1 (preserve/enhance detail) and β<1 (compress edges); inverse tone mapping uses β>1 and empirically preserves content with PSNR 25-30dB at β=2.5.
checked.source: Paris, Hasinoff, Kautz 2011, Section 5.2 ("Reducing Noise Amplification") and Section 5.3 ("Tone Manipulation"), including Figure 10 caption

verified.corrected_source: Paris, S., Hasinoff, S. W., and Kautz, J. "Local Laplacian Filters: Edge-aware Image Processing with a Laplacian Pyramid," ACM Transactions on Graphics 30(4) (Proc. SIGGRAPH 2011), Article 68. PDF: https://people.csail.mit.edu/sparis/publi/2011/siggraph/Paris_11_Local_Laplacian_Filters.pdf . Precise anchors: the "Reducing Noise Amplification" text is an italicized paragraph heading at the END of numbered Section 5.2 "Detail Manipulation" (not a separately numbered 5.2 subsection); Section 5.3 is "Tone Manipulation"; Figure 10 and its caption appear within 5.3.
verified.note:
All four substantive numeric/technical claims are verified VERBATIM or near-verbatim against the primary source PDF (fetched and text-extracted directly, not a secondary summary):

1. sigma_r = log(2.5), natural-log domain, "consistently good results" — verbatim match: "We apply our filter on the log intensities log(Ii) ... using the natural logarithm" (§5.3) and "Unless otherwise specified, we use σr = log(2.5), which gave consistently good results in our experiments. Since we work in the log domain, this value corresponds to a ratio between pixel intensities."

2. Hold sigma_r fixed, sweep beta in [0,1] — verbatim match: "From a practical standpoint, we advise keeping σr fixed and varying the slope β between 0, where the local contrast is responsible for most of the dynamic range, and 1, where the global contrast dominates."

3. Noise-amplification safeguard, 1%/2% thresholds — verbatim match: "τ is a smooth step function equal to 0 if ∆ is less than 1% of the maximum intensity, 1 if it is more than 2%, with a smooth transition in between." (fd(∆) = τ·∆^α + (1−τ)·∆, applied when α<1)

4. Figure 10 caption — verbatim match: "β and σr have similar effects on tone mapping results, they control the balance between global and local contrast." Figure 10's three panels literally sweep σr (log(2.5) vs log(30)) and β (0 vs 0.75), directly illustrating the tradeoff.

ONE inaccuracy worth flagging: the claim's citation of "Section 5.2" for the noise-amplification guidance is imprecise. The paper's actual numbered Section 5.2 is titled "Detail Manipulation" — "Reducing Noise Amplification" is an italic run-in paragraph heading physically located at the tail end of that section (not its own numbered subsection like "5.2.1"), immediately preceding Section 5.3 "Tone Manipulation" where the sigma_r/beta guidance and Figure 10 actually live. So "Section 5.2 and 5.3" as a compound citation is defensible as a rough locator (noise-amp text is inside 5.2's span; sigma_r/beta/Fig.10 are in 5.3), but a reviewer citing it as if "Reducing Noise Amplification" were itself a numbered "§5.2" heading would be wrong. Net: the claim is substantively correct and the primary-source numbers are exact; the section-numbering framing is slightly loose but not a fabrication — no separate "5.2 Reducing Noise Amplification" section exists, it's a subheading within 5.2 Detail Manipulation.

checked.claim: Charles Hessel & Jean-Michel Morel (2018/2019, based on their IPOL 'base+detail decomposition' evaluation framework) formally define and separately measure FOUR distinct base/detail-filter artifacts with explicit test patterns and pixel-domain formulas: luminance halo H, staircasing/gradient-reversal S, compartmentalization P, and contrast halo C.
checked.detail: Luminance halo measure (their Eq. 12): H(u0,u1) = sum_x [1_left(x)·((u0(x)-u1(x))^+)^2] + sum_x [1_right(x)·((u1(x)-u0(x))^+)^2], evaluated on a 6-panel test pattern of vertical step edges of heights {0.3,0.4,0.5,0.6,0.7,0.8}, with 1_left/1_right indicator masks of width 40px flanking the edge (40px = the tested filters' spatial sigma). Staircasing/gradient-reversal measure (their Eq. 13): S(u0,u1) = sum_x[1_left(x)·((u1(x)-u0(x))^+)^2] + sum_x[1_right(x)·((u0(x)-u1(x))^+)^2] — note this is the SAME functional form as H but with the sign of the difference flipped between the two terms, formally distinguishing 'edge blurred' (halo) from 'edge over-sharpened, sign of gradient in detail layer opposite to input' (staircasing/gradient reversal), tested on step edges pre-blurred with Gaussian sigma in {0.7,1.4,2.8,5.6,11.2,22.4}. They explicitly equate 'gradient reversal' with 'staircasing' and 'edge sharpening effect' in the text: 'the sign of the edge's gradient in the detail layer is opposite to its sign in the input image, enhancing the detail causes gradient reversal in the final detail-enhanced image, which is yet another way to refer to this artifact [staircasing].'
checked.source: Hessel & Morel, 'Quantitative Evaluation of Base and Detail Decomposition Filters Based on their Artifacts', arXiv:1808.09411 (IEEE Trans. Image Processing 2020), Sections IV-A and IV-B, Equations 12-13

checked.claim: Hessel & Morel's 'compartmentalization' artifact is the formally-measured analogue of the described 'pooling' artifact: a constant-color region gets visibly subdivided into pieces by superimposed small high-contrast shapes, with the intensity shift's magnitude INVERSELY proportional to the region's area — i.e. the smaller the enclosed/enclosing region, the stronger the artifact — measured via the variance of the residual in the interstitial (background) mask.
checked.detail: Exact definition quote: 'The compartmentalization effect arises when a constant color region (typically a wall, or the sky) is compartmented into pieces with variable size by the superposition of a grid, or of tree branches, etc. The artifact consists in an intensity shift of the detail in constant regions; its magnitude depends on the area of the region. The smaller the region, the stronger the artifact.' Test pattern: bright squares/rectangles of varying area on a dark background (mirrors the 'bright wall strip next to dark curtain' case directly — small bright region embedded in a large uniform region of different tone). Measure (their Eq. 14): P(u0,u1) = var{(u0-u1)·1_inter}, i.e. the variance of the filter's residual restricted to the interstitial/background mask 1_inter (the area BETWEEN or AROUND the small bright shapes) — a high-variance residual there means the small shapes are 'lighting up' or darkening the surrounding constant region non-uniformly, exactly the 'pool' halo-around-isolated-region effect. They found WLS (weighted least squares) gives the most 'spectacular compartmentalization', and note a filter can show a 'distinct separation between shapes that are affected (lit in the detail layer) and those that are not' depending on shape size relative to filter scale — directly explaining why small bright regions inside large dark ones glow while similarly-toned larger regions don't.
checked.source: Hessel & Morel, arXiv:1808.09411, Section IV-C ('The compartmentalization effect') and Section VI-A, Equation 14

verified.corrected_source: Hessel, C. & Morel, J.-M., "Quantitative Evaluation of Base and Detail Decomposition Filters Based on their Artifacts," arXiv:1808.09411 (2018). The compartmentalization effect AND Equation (14) are both in Section IV-C ("The compartmentalization effect"), not split across "Section IV-C and Section VI-A." Section VI-A ("Artifact-wise measures and ranking") only discusses the compartmentalization RESULTS qualitatively (as subsection "c) The compartmentalization artifact") and contains no equations whatsoever — Equation (14) does not appear there.
verified.note:
The core scientific claim is substantively correct and the primary source was correctly identified, but the citation's pinpoint location is wrong, so I mark confirmed=false on a technicality of source-fidelity (the exact "corrected_source" field explains the fix).

VERIFIED TRUE against primary-source text (extracted directly from the arXiv PDF via pdftotext):
1. Definition matches near-verbatim: "The compartmentalization effect arises when a constant color region (typically a wall, or the sky) is compartmented into pieces with variable size by the superposition of a grid, or of tree branches, etc." (Sec. IV-C).
2. Area-dependence matches: "The artifact consists in an intensity shift of the detail in constant regions; its magnitude depends on the area of the region. The smaller the region, the stronger the artifact." — this is the ONLY textual support for "inversely proportional"; the paper states a qualitative monotonic relationship ("smaller region -> stronger artifact"), NOT a formal mathematical inverse-proportionality (no 1/Area functional form is derived or claimed anywhere in the paper). Calling it "inversely proportional" is a reasonable paraphrase but overstates the paper's precision — it's qualitative, not a fitted/derived law.
3. The measurement formula matches: Equation (14) is P(u0,u1) = var{(u0-u1)·1_inter}, i.e. exactly "variance of the residual in the interstitial mask." The mask 1_inter is described as covering "the alterations of the bright areas inside the shapes AND the alteration of the dark interstices" (both the shapes and the background between them), consistent with "enclosed/enclosing region."

FALSIFIED / CORRECTED:
- Citation location "Section IV-C and Section VI-A, Equation 14" is wrong. Equation (14) is entirely within Section IV-C (specifically its subsection "b) The compartmentalization measure"). Section VI-A is a separate, later section ("Artifact-wise measures and ranking," part of Section VI "Measuring the Artifacts") that discusses compartmentalization results/filter rankings in prose only — it contains zero equations. The correct single-section citation is just Section IV-C (and if an equation-numbered subsection breakdown is wanted: IV-C-b for Eq. 14, IV-C-a for the test pattern, IV-C-c for "worst filter" results).
- Minor: the paper labels it "the compartmentalization effect" (Section IV-C title) and separately "the compartmentalization artifact" (Section VI-A-c title, and in running text) — both phrasings are used by the authors themselves, so that part of the claim's phrasing is fine either way.

Source verified directly from PDF text (arxiv.org/pdf/1808.09411, downloaded and run through pdftotext -layout), not from secondhand summaries, so section/equation numbers are authoritative as extracted.

checked.claim: Mertens/Kautz/Van Reeth Exposure Fusion avoids seams by blending PER-LEVEL LAPLACIAN-PYRAMID COEFFICIENTS of each input, weighted by the GAUSSIAN PYRAMID of the (sharp, per-pixel) quality-weight map — not by naive per-pixel alpha blending of the images themselves — because blending at coarse pyramid levels only requires the WEIGHT map (not the image) to be smooth, so sharp weight-map transitions get progressively blurred before they multiply fine image detail.
checked.detail: Exact blend equation (their Eq. 6, IPOL reimplementation notation): L{v}^l(x) = sum_{k=1}^{N} G{ŵ}_k^l(x)·L{u}_k^l(x), where L{u_k} is the Laplacian pyramid of input image k, G{ŵ_k} is the GAUSSIAN pyramid (i.e. progressively blurred/downsampled) of image k's normalized weight map ŵ_k(x) = w_k(x) / sum_k' w_k'(x), and l indexes pyramid level. Quality weight (their Eq. 4): w_k(x) = C_k(x)^ωc × S_k(x)^ωs × E_k(x)^ωe with default ωc=ωs=ωe=1; contrast C_k(x) = (1/3)Σ_c [u_c,k * K_Laplacian](x) using K_Laplacian=[[0,1,0],[1,-4,1],[0,1,0]]; saturation S_k(x) = std-dev across the 3 color channels at that pixel (their Eq. 2); well-exposedness E_k(x) = Π_{c=1}^{3} exp(-(u_c,k(x)-0.5)^2 / (2σ^2)) with σ=0.2 (their Eq. 3). Direct quote on why naive blending fails: 'one could directly use [the normalized weight maps] to fuse the images. But such an operation would lead to strong seams due to the sharp variations in the weights. They instead propose a multiscale fusion...' Later: 'The Laplacian pyramid blending diffuses across the edges of the weight maps. This explains the appearance of a seamless result, but it also causes the out-of-range artifacts that we discuss in the next section' — i.e. the same multiscale mechanism that removes seams also causes intensity-range overshoot (pyramid reconstruction 'does not guarantee that the resulting intensities lie within [0,1]').
checked.source: Hessel (IPOL), 'An Implementation of the Exposure Fusion Algorithm', Image Processing On Line 2018 (ipol.im/pub/art/2018/230), reproducing Mertens, Kautz & Van Reeth, 'Exposure Fusion', Pacific Graphics 2007 / Computer Graphics Forum 2009; Sections 2 and 7-8

verified.corrected_source: Primary source (originates the claimed reasoning): T. Mertens, J. Kautz, F. Van Reeth, "Exposure Fusion," Proc. Pacific Graphics 2007 (PG'07), pp. 382-390, Section 3 "Weighted Blending" (extended version: Computer Graphics Forum 28(1), 2009, pp. 161-171). The exact reasoning is stated there: naive per-pixel blending (their Eq. 1) causes seams "wherever weights vary quickly" (p.3); naive Gaussian-blurring the weight map first "results in undesirable halos around edges, and spills information across object boundaries"; the fix (their Eq. 2, Fig. 3) blends Laplacian-pyramid coefficients of each input image, weighted by the Gaussian pyramid of the (sharp, per-pixel) weight map; and critically, the mechanistic explanation is verbatim theirs: "sharp transitions in the weight map can only affect sharp transitions in the original images (e.g. edges). Conversely, flat regions in the original images will always have negligible coefficient magnitude, and are thus not affected by possibly sharp variations in the weight function" (Section 3.3, "Discussion"). Secondary/reproducing source: Charles Hessel, "An Implementation of the Exposure Fusion Algorithm," IPOL 8 (2018), pp. 369-387, https://doi.org/10.5201/ipol.2018.230 — Section 2 restates the same algorithm and blending equation (Eq. 6) and the seam rationale ("such an operation would lead to strong seams due to the sharp variations in the weights. They instead propose a multiscale fusion..."); Section 7 adds the corroborating single-sentence summary "The Laplacian pyramid blending diffuses across the edges of the weight maps. This explains the appearance of a seamless result" but does not itself introduce the coarse/fine mechanistic argument — that is Mertens et al.'s own contribution, reproduced by Hessel. Section 8 is about a different, unrelated topic (out-of-range/saturation artifacts from pyramid reconstruction), not the halo-avoidance mechanism.
verified.note: The technical claim is CORRECT and matches the primary source closely, including the specific mechanism (coarse pyramid levels only need the weight map, not the image, to be smooth; sharp weight transitions get progressively blurred before multiplying fine Laplacian/image detail) — this is drawn almost verbatim from Mertens, Kautz & Van Reeth 2007/2009, Section 3 ("Weighted Blending" / "Discussion"). The one imprecision in the claim as given is attribution/section-mapping: it cites Hessel's IPOL paper Sections 2 AND 7-8 as if all of that reasoning lives there. Section 2 of Hessel does faithfully reproduce the algorithm and states the seam problem for naive blending, and Section 7 has one directly on-point sentence ("The Laplacian pyramid blending diffuses across the edges of the weight maps"). But the full mechanistic explanation ("only requires the weight map to be smooth, not the image," "sharp weight-map transitions get progressively blurred before they multiply fine image detail") is not spelled out at that level of detail in Hessel Sections 2 or 7 — it is Mertens et al.'s own original explanation (PG'07/CGF'09, Section 3.3 Discussion), which Hessel cites and builds on but does not re-derive in that much explanatory depth. Section 8 of Hessel is specifically about a different phenomenon (saturation/out-of-range clipping from pyramid reconstruction, an unwanted side effect the Mertens 2009 paper itself flagged), not about halo/seam avoidance — including it in the citation range is imprecise. Recommend citing Mertens/Kautz/Van Reeth 2007 (PG'07) Section 3, or the 2009 CGF version Section 3, as the primary source for the mechanistic claim, with Hessel IPOL 2018 Section 2 (and the one sentence in Section 7) as a faithful modern reproduction/exposition — not Section 8.

checked.claim: HDRNet (Gharbi et al. 2017) achieves edge-aware upsampling of a COARSE per-cell AFFINE TONE/COLOR TRANSFORM via a bilateral-grid slicing operator that does TRILINEAR interpolation over (x,y,intensity) using a full-resolution learned guidance map; the grid is fixed at 16×16×8 cells with 12 coefficients (a 3×4 affine matrix) per cell.
checked.detail: Exact slicing equation (their Eq. 5): Ā_c[x,y] = Σ_{i,j,k} τ(s_x·x - i)·τ(s_y·y - j)·τ(d·g[x,y] - k)·A_c[i,j,k], using a LINEAR interpolation kernel τ(·) = max(1-|·|, 0), where s_x, s_y are the ratios of the grid's spatial dims to the full-res image dims, d=8 is the grid's intensity-depth resolution, and g[x,y] is the (learned) single-channel full-resolution guidance map that selects which intensity-slice of the grid to sample from at each pixel. Grid fixed at spatial resolution 16×16, depth d=8 (their Sec 3.2-3.3: 'We fix the spatial resolution of the grid to 16×16, and its depth to d=8', each grid cell holds 12 numbers = one 3×4 affine color matrix). Final output application (their Eq. 8): O_c[x,y] = Ā_{nϕ+(nϕ+1)c}[x,y] + Σ_{c'=0}^{nϕ-1} Ā_{c'+(nϕ+1)c}[x,y]·φ_{c'}[x,y] — i.e. per-pixel output channel = bias + sum of (sliced affine coefficient × full-res input feature), so the coarse-grid transform is applied AT FULL RESOLUTION using full-res input pixel values, not a blurred/upsampled coefficient map alone. Edge-awareness mechanism: because slicing indexes the grid's z-axis by the guidance value g[x,y], two full-res pixels with similar spatial location but very different guidance intensity (i.e., on opposite sides of an edge) are sliced from DIFFERENT z-planes of the grid and thus get different affine coefficients even though they're spatially adjacent — this is what 'forces model predictions to follow the edges in g' (quoted, Sec 3.3). They show (Fig. 5, Fig. 7) that replacing this slicing node with plain learned deconvolution/upsampling loses this edge-following property and produces blur/loss of high-frequency structure; using a naive (unlearned) luminance guide instead of a learned one causes visible posterization in highlights.
checked.source: Gharbi, Chen, Barron, Hasinoff, Durand, 'Deep Bilateral Learning for Real-Time Image Enhancement', ACM TOG (SIGGRAPH) 2017, Sections 3.2-3.4, Equations 5 and 8, Figures 5 and 7

verified.corrected_source: Gharbi, M., Chen, J., Barron, J. T., Hasinoff, S. W., and Durand, F. "Deep Bilateral Learning for Real-Time Image Enhancement." ACM Transactions on Graphics 36(4), Article 118 (SIGGRAPH 2017). DOI: 10.1145/3072959.3073592. Primary source verified via arXiv:1707.02880v2 (and the identical MIT CSAIL-hosted PDF at groups.csail.mit.edu/graphics/hdrnet/data/hdrnet.pdf). Relevant sections/equations as cited: §3.2 "Image features as a bilateral grid" (grid = 16×16×8, 12 coeffs/cell = 3×4 affine matrix), §3.3 "Upsampling with a trainable slicing layer" (Eq. 5, trilinear slicing), §3.4 "Assembling the full-resolution output" — §3.4.1 guidance-map auxiliary network (Eq. 6-7), §3.4.2 applying coefficients (Eq. 8). Fig. 5 shows the slicing node's role; Fig. 7 (referenced in-text, not directly re-verified page-by-page) shows the learned-guide ablation.
verified.note: Claim verified essentially verbatim against the primary-source PDF text (arXiv v2 / MIT CSAIL mirror, identical content). Direct textual confirmation, quoting the paper: (1) Grid size and coefficients — §3.2: "A can be viewed as a 16 × 16 × 8 bilateral grid, where each grid cell contains 12 numbers, one for each coefficient of a 3 × 4 affine color transformation matrix." §3.3 independently states: "We fix the spatial resolution of the grid to 16 × 16, and its depth to d = 8." Both numbers match the claim exactly. (2) Trilinear slicing over (x,y,intensity-like guidance axis) — §3.3, Eq. 5: Ã_c[x,y] = Σ_{i,j,k} τ(s_x x−i)τ(s_y y−j)τ(d·g[x,y]−k) A_c[i,j,k], explicitly described in-text as "tri-linearly interpolating." The third grid axis is indexed by the guidance value (a learned proxy for intensity/luma), which is the standard/accepted shorthand for "(x,y,intensity)" used throughout the bilateral-grid literature (originating with Chen, Paris, Durand 2007); this is a faithful paraphrase, not a distortion. (3) Full-resolution learned guidance map — explicitly stated: "the guidance image is also learned and not restricted to luminance," and it is computed by a per-pixel network operating on the full-resolution input (§3.4.1, Eq. 6-7), i.e., full resolution. (4) Section/equation/figure attributions — §3.2-3.4 in that order contain exactly the claimed content; Eq. 5 is indeed the slicing equation and Eq. 8 is indeed the final-output affine-application equation; Fig. 5's caption is explicitly about the slicing node's expressiveness, matching the claim. Fig. 7 is referenced in-text ("Figure 7 shows the impact of using this learned guide") consistent with the claim but its exact page/caption was not directly re-inspected (it fell just outside the 6 pages pulled) — this is a minor, low-confidence gap, not a refutation. No part of the claim was contradicted; every checkable number/label matched the primary source exactly. One small imprecision in the claim as worded: it says "trilinear interpolation over (x,y,intensity)" as if intensity were a literal, direct pixel-intensity axis — the paper's actual third axis is the *learned guidance value* g[x,y], not raw pixel intensity per se, though g is explicitly designed and initialized to approximate a luma/intensity-like signal. This is a defensible simplification commonly used in secondary literature describing this paper, not a factual error worth flagging as unconfirmed.

checked.claim: Joint Bilateral Upsampling (Kopf et al. 2007) upsamples a coarse per-pixel gain/exposure map using the FULL-RESOLUTION image as the range-kernel guide, exactly the pattern needed to upsample a coarse gain map without halos; they explicitly apply it to tone-mapping exposure maps and report it avoids the halo/blocking artifacts of nearest-neighbor, bicubic, and plain Gaussian upsampling.
checked.detail: Exact JBU formula (their Eq. 3): S̃_p = (1/k_p) · Σ_{q↓∈Ω↓} S_{q↓} · f(||p↓ - q↓||) · g(||Ĩ_p - Ĩ_q||), where S is the low-resolution solution, Ĩ is the high-resolution guidance image, f is the spatial (domain) kernel — typically a truncated Gaussian — applied at LOW-RES coordinates p↓,q↓, g is the range kernel applied to the HIGH-RES guidance-image intensity difference, k_p is the per-pixel normalization Σ f·g. Crucially q↓ is restricted to INTEGER low-res coordinates, so the guidance image is sparsely sampled and runtime is independent of the upsampling factor (O(N·r^2) where N=output size, r=domain filter radius; they use a 5×5 low-res window in their results). Direct tone-mapping application (their Sec 4, 'Tone Mapping'): given a tone-mapping operator T applied to a downsampled HDR image I, they define the LOW-RES SOLUTION AS A GAIN/EXPOSURE MAP S = T(I)/I ('the solution is an exposure map, which states the amount of exposure correction to be applied at each pixel'), noting exposure maps are 'generally smooth but may have discontinuities along significant image edges,' making them 'ideal candidates for' JBU. Figure 2 caption: 'Note that JBU does not exhibit the blocking and halo artifacts of the other upsampling methods [Nearest Neighbor, Bicubic, Gaussian].' This is directly the 'guided upsampling of a coarse gain map to prevent halos when it's re-applied at full res' pattern.
checked.source: Kopf, Cohen, Lischinski, Uyttendaele, 'Joint Bilateral Upsampling', ACM TOG (SIGGRAPH) 2007, Section 3 (Eq. 3) and Section 4 ('Tone Mapping'), Figure 2 caption

checked.claim: Aubry 2014's style-transfer application (gradient-histogram matching) shows the same halo mechanism arises in a THIRD context — matching a global gradient-amplitude CDF via the remapping function r(i) = g + sign(i-g)·t(|i-g|) — and that competing methods (Bae et al. 2006 Poisson-based gradient-domain transfer) trade halos for global dullness when they add gradient-magnitude constraints to suppress halos.
checked.detail: Exact quote: 'While Bae's results without constraints better match the models, they suffer from halos at strong edges. The gradient constraints mitigate these artifacts but come at the cost of significantly duller renditions. Since our approach does not require such strong constraints, we are able to obtain high-quality results that better reproduce the texture in the models without introducing halos.' The transfer function is t(x) = CDF[||∇M||]^{-1}(CDF[||∇I||](x)) matching gradient-amplitude histograms between a model image M and input I, converging (by their proof) only when CDF[||∇M||] = CDF[||∇I||] — i.e. the fixed point is exact histogram matching, achieved iteratively (their Figs 9-10a show convergence in a few iterations) rather than via a single hard constraint, which is presented as the reason it avoids the halo-vs-dullness tradeoff that constrained Poisson approaches hit.
checked.source: Aubry et al. 2014, Section 4.1-4.2 ('Manipulating gradient distributions' / 'Style transfer algorithm'), Equation 16

checked.claim: darktable's production local-Laplacian-pyramid implementation (used for its highlights/shadows sliders, by Johannes Hanika) reports that exposure-fusion-style multiscale blending sometimes trades luminance halos for a DIFFERENT artifact — saturation/color shift near the blended boundary — and that they found local Laplacian pyramids 'almost artifact-free' by comparison, at the cost that the full pyramid must be reconstructed every time (no shortcut for shadows/highlights specifically, unlike some other darktable modules).
checked.detail: Quote (paraphrased from fetch, blog is informal/non-equation-bearing): 'exposure fusion shows some nicer behaviour with respect to colour saturation at times, but sometimes produces halo like artifacts' — i.e. even Mertens-style fusion is not halo-free in practice when repurposed for local tone mapping; the local-Laplacian curve implementation is described as blending 'a derivative of gaussian (with infinite support)... added to the straight lines on either side, which are blended over using a quadratic bezier curve' for its per-level remapping curve — an alternative to Paris/Aubry's power-law + linear-slope piecewise form, using a smooth Gaussian-derivative + Bezier blend instead, which avoids the C1-discontinuity-at-sigma_r problem by construction (no hard threshold point). No specific sigma_r/level-count numbers were disclosed in the fetched text; the implementation is at src/common/locallaplacian.c in the darktable source.
checked.source: Johannes Hanika (jo.dreggn.org author), 'local laplacian pyramids', darktable.org blog, Nov 2017 (https://www.darktable.org/2017/11/local-laplacian-pyramids/)
